tEn: Sam Altman on AI, Parenthood, and the Future
tZh: Sam Altman 谈 AI、育儿与未来
sEn: Sam Altman discusses the pros and cons of AI, his new role as a father, and the future of technology.
sZh: Sam Altman 探讨 AI 的利弊、初为人父的体验以及科技的未来。

consensus.en: Kids will be fine with AI; parents need to adapt to new technology.
consensus.zh: 孩子能适应 AI，父母才需要调整适应新技术。

consensus.en: AI will eliminate some jobs but create new ones, as history shows.
consensus.zh: AI 会淘汰一些工作，但也会创造新工作，历史已证明。

consensus.en: Human purpose and creativity will persist even as AI advances.
consensus.zh: 即使 AI 进步，人类的目的和创造力仍会持续。

consensus.en: Privacy and legal frameworks for AI conversations need urgent attention.
consensus.zh: AI 对话的隐私和法律框架亟需关注。

consensus.en: AI should help people achieve their goals, not just entertain them.
consensus.zh: AI 应帮助人们实现目标，而非仅仅娱乐。

consensus.en: Nuclear fusion is needed to meet future energy demands for AI.
consensus.zh: 核聚变是满足未来 AI 能源需求的关键。

contrarian.en: AI could become an AI CEO of OpenAI, and that's awesome, not sad.
contrarian.zh: AI 可能成为 OpenAI 的 CEO，这很棒，而非可悲。

contrarian.en: Universal basic wealth is better than universal basic income.
contrarian.zh: 全民基本财富优于全民基本收入。

contrarian.en: AI may help answer questions about God and the nature of the universe.
contrarian.zh: AI 可能帮助解答关于上帝和宇宙本质的问题。

contrarian.en: The 'merge' with AI may happen through imprinting patterns, not implants.
contrarian.zh: 与 AI 的融合可能通过模式印记实现，而非植入物。

contrarian.en: Surveillance for safety is dystopic even if it reduces crime.
contrarian.zh: 为安全而监控即使减少犯罪也是反乌托邦的。

contrarian.en: Learning to love hard parts and tension is a key to happiness.
contrarian.zh: 学会热爱困难和紧张是幸福的关键。

turns.en: Today's guest is a straight-up tech lord, let's be honest. He's one of the world leaders in the development of AI. He started OpenAI, which is known for having ChatGPT. We had a fascinating chat about the pros and cons, the fears and hopes, everything I could learn about artificial intelligence and where we're headed. TBD, baby. Today's guest is Mr. Sam Altman, and I'm very thankful for his time.
turns.zh: 今天的嘉宾是名副其实的科技巨头，毫不夸张。他是全球人工智能开发的领军人物之一，创立了以 ChatGPT 闻名的 OpenAI。我们聊了很多，关于利弊、恐惧与希望，以及我能学到的关于人工智能和未来走向的一切。未来待定，宝贝。今天的嘉宾是 Sam Altman 先生，非常感谢他抽出时间。

turns.en: And I will find a song. I will sing it.
turns.zh: 我会找一首歌，然后唱出来。

turns.en: You know, we had a residential architect do this office. We wanted it to feel like someone's really comfortable country house or something like that.
turns.zh: 你知道吗，我们请了住宅建筑师来设计这个办公室。我们希望它感觉像某人非常舒适的乡间别墅之类的。

turns.en: Yeah. Not like the big corporate sci-fi castle.
turns.zh: 对，不像那种大型企业科幻城堡。

turns.en: Yeah, that's what I was thinking. I was a little like, oh, is it going to be, you know, will there be a drawbridge? Will we be uploaded into a suit? Like what will happen to us?
turns.zh: 是啊，我当时就在想，哦，会不会有吊桥？我们会不会被上传到套装里？我们会遭遇什么？

turns.en: You don't want that. We're going for residential.
turns.zh: 你不会想要那样的。我们走的是住宅风格。

turns.en: Yeah. I was like, how do we even get through the firewall? How many hit points will we need to get through? You know, it got very Dungeons and Dragons in some of my imagination sometimes.
turns.zh: 是啊。我当时想，我们怎么穿过防火墙？需要多少生命值才能通过？你知道，我的想象力有时候会变得很像《龙与地下城》。

turns.en: Yeah, we want people to feel super comfortable and tried to get pretty far in that direction.
turns.zh: 对，我们希望人们感到非常舒适，并且在这方面做得相当到位。

turns.en: It feels like it. And your staff's very sweet, nice people. Thanks for hanging out, man.
turns.zh: 确实感觉如此。你的员工也非常友善、亲切。谢谢你陪我聊天，伙计。

turns.en: Absolutely. Thanks, I appreciate it. Yeah, I haven't seen you since I fell out of my chair.
turns.zh: 不客气。谢谢，我很感激。是啊，自从我从椅子上摔下来之后就没见过你了。

turns.en: Fell out of a chair at the inauguration.
turns.zh: 在就职典礼上从椅子上摔下来。

turns.en: That was really quite a way to meet you.
turns.zh: 那真是一种特别的见面方式。

turns.en: Yeah, I felt so embarrassed. And you were one of the faces that I looked up and saw and I was like, 'God.' And that was my first moment like, 'AI, build us a better chair,' to be honest with you.
turns.zh: 是啊，我觉得特别尴尬。我抬头看到你的脸，心想，‘天哪。’那是我第一次想，‘AI，给我们造把更好的椅子吧。’说实话。

turns.en: You did nothing, right? You were just sitting there and it just collapsed. Nothing.
turns.zh: 你什么都没做，对吧？你就坐在那儿，椅子就塌了。什么都没做。

turns.en: I remember that.
turns.zh: 我记得。

turns.en: And it was just so embarrassing. I was like, 'Oh, of all people me and here I am in this place.'
turns.zh: 而且真是太尴尬了。我当时想，‘哦，偏偏是我，在这个地方。’

turns.en: I think it was perfect because everybody's got to have some story when people ask, 'Oh, was the inauguration like?' Everybody's got to have some story to tell.
turns.zh: 我觉得这很完美，因为当别人问‘就职典礼怎么样？’时，每个人都需要有个故事可讲。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: And that was an incredible story for us all to tell.
turns.zh: 而这对我们所有人来说都是一个不可思议的故事。

turns.en: That's a good point. I do remember looking at people for help, though. And oddly, your eyes I was like, 'Oh my god, he could help.' You did look like a beacon of help in the distance.
turns.zh: 说得好。不过我确实记得当时在向人们求助。奇怪的是，你的眼神让我觉得，‘哦天哪，他能帮忙。’你看起来就像远处的一座希望灯塔。

turns.en: I tried to help.
turns.zh: 我确实想帮忙来着。

turns.en: You have a baby. You have a new child.
turns.zh: 你有了一个宝宝。你有了一个孩子。

turns.en: It is. There have been a lot of experiences in life where everyone tells you something's going to be great and then it's like, okay, the people are right, the consensus is right, it's even better than I thought it was going to be. But this has been the strongest example of that ever.
turns.zh: 是的。生活中有很多经历，每个人都告诉你某件事会很棒，然后你发现，好吧，人们是对的，共识是对的，它比我想象的还要好。但这绝对是迄今为止最强烈的例子。

turns.en: Like I knew it was going to be great and it's way way better. It's impossible to describe. There's nothing I can say that's not very cliche and it's totally amazing.
turns.zh: 就像我知道它会很棒，但它要好得多得多。无法形容。我说不出任何不陈词滥调的话，但它绝对令人惊叹。

turns.en: What is one of the things that brings you joy with it? You have a young boy. What's something that you think is neat?
turns.zh: 其中让你感到快乐的一件事是什么？你有一个小男孩。你觉得什么很酷？

turns.en: Watching the speed with which he learns new things or gains new capabilities. It's just unbelievable. Every day it's like, oh man, he just couldn't do that before and now he's grabbing stuff and passing it between his hands. Getting to watch it day to day is just an amazing rate of change. And then I realize that everything about babies is very finely tuned over a long period of evolution to make us love them and be fascinated by them. It's like a neurochemical hack. But I love it. It's great. It's so strong. It's so intense.
turns.zh: 看着他学习新事物或获得新能力的速度。简直难以置信。每天都会想，哦天哪，他之前还做不到，现在却能抓东西并在双手间传递。日复一日地观察这种变化，速度惊人。然后我意识到，婴儿的一切都是经过漫长进化精心调校的，让我们爱他们、被他们吸引。这就像一种神经化学 hack。但我喜欢这种感觉。太棒了。如此强烈，如此深刻。

turns.en: So it's really like almost like a coffee for your heart or something. I don't even know how to find an analogy. I've tried to come up with one to tell because now I'm telling everybody you've got to have a lot of kids. It's really important.
turns.zh: 所以这真的有点像给心脏来杯咖啡之类的。我甚至不知道怎么找比喻。我试过想一个来说，因为现在我告诉每个人，你必须多生孩子。这真的很重要。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。是啊。是啊。

turns.en: And I've been looking for an analogy to explain, and then I always just say I don't know how to explain this. It's just the best thing I've ever done by far. I feel like a completely changed person. I was thinking the other day, there used to be all these other things. At this point, all I do is work and hang out with my family. I don't really get to do a lot of hobbies anymore. It's busy time at work. I don't get to hang out with my friends that much. And there were all these things where people tell you, 'Oh, you got to do this before the baby comes, take that spontaneous international trip because you're not going to be doing that again for a long time.' And I was like, 'Oh, that is kind of sad.' In practice, you don't do it that often. I at least didn't do it that often, and I don't miss it at all. I remember that used to be a possibility. Now I can see that's not going to be a possibility for a long time, and I'm thrilled with the trade.
turns.zh: 我一直在找一个比喻来解释，但最后总是说我不知道怎么解释。这绝对是我做过的最棒的事。我感觉自己完全变了一个人。前几天我在想，以前还有那么多别的事情。现在，我所有的时间就是工作和陪家人。我不再有很多爱好。工作很忙。我也不怎么和朋友聚会了。人们总说，‘哦，你得在孩子出生前做这个，来一次说走就走的国际旅行，因为之后很长一段时间你都做不了了。’我当时想，‘哦，那有点可惜。’但实际上，你本来也不常做。至少我不常做，而且我一点也不怀念。我记得那曾经是一种可能。现在我知道很长一段时间内都不可能了，但我对这个交换感到非常兴奋。

turns.en: You've moved on.
turns.zh: 你已经向前看了。

turns.en: I'm so happy.
turns.zh: 我太开心了。

turns.en: How old is your child?
turns.zh: 你的孩子多大了？

turns.en: Four months. Oh, that's a funny age. At five or six months they start to get fun, and you can like, they're still like they can't go anywhere, you know, but they're intrigued and stuff. They start to smile or process more. I don't know how you guys say it, but yeah, he's totally turned on though, really aware, understands things. It's super cool.
turns.zh: 四个月。哦，那是个有趣的年龄。到五六个月的时候他们开始变得好玩，虽然还是不能到处跑，但他们会好奇之类的。他们开始微笑或处理更多信息。我不知道你们怎么说，但总之他完全“开启”了，非常警觉，能理解东西。超级酷。

turns.en: I have a thought sometimes that this will be one of the last, maybe 40 years, that we conceive children in the body. Did you have any thoughts about that? I've definitely heard a lot of people say that. I haven't thought about it hard myself, but yeah, I guess it does make sense. Like I guess that does make sense.
turns.zh: 我有时会想，这可能是最后几十年，我们还在体内孕育孩子。你有什么想法吗？我确实听过很多人这么说。我自己没有深入思考过，但嗯，我想这确实有道理。我想确实有道理。

turns.en: Like, God, you were in your mom's butt. It's crazy. You know, you pervert or whatever. I think in the future people will do it in a vet or something.
turns.zh: 就像，天哪，你在你妈妈的屁股里。太疯狂了。你知道，你是个变态之类的。我觉得未来人们会在类似兽医诊所的地方做这件事。

turns.en: Yes. In like a nice vet. You can go see it on the weekends or whatever.
turns.zh: 对。在一个不错的兽医诊所。你可以周末去看看它之类的。

turns.en: Doesn't that just feel off to you? Like I can totally intellectually understand that may be the better way to do it.
turns.zh: 你不觉得这有点不对劲吗？我完全能在理智上理解那可能是更好的方式。

turns.en: Oh yeah. It feels way off to me.
turns.zh: 哦是啊。我觉得很不对劲。

turns.en: I thought you would like it, you know? I thought that would be like a futuristic thought. I can totally accept that that will be what everybody does and that it's easier and we can make it healthier for the child and the mother. The mother doesn't take the health risk. But emotionally it feels like something is off.
turns.zh: 我觉得你会喜欢这个，你知道吗？我觉得那会是一个未来主义的想法。我完全可以接受那将成为每个人的做法，而且它更简单，我们可以让孩子和母亲都更健康。母亲不用承担健康风险。但情感上感觉有点不对劲。

turns.en: Oh yeah. Yeah. Because then the family, on the weekends the parents would come and tinker on the glass or whatever, or the dad would put a Go-Falcons sticker on the thing. People would decorate it all up or write little messages on there.
turns.zh: 哦，是的。是的。因为那样的话，周末父母会过来在玻璃上捣鼓什么的，或者爸爸会在上面贴个猎鹰队贴纸。人们会把它装饰起来，或者在上面写点小留言。

turns.en: I think there's another take on all of this: in this world we're heading to, with crazy sci-fi technology becoming reality, the deeply human things will become the most precious, sacred, valued things. And we'll really care about the human experience more than ever. Maybe it won't go that way, I don't know.
turns.zh: 我对这一切还有另一种看法：在我们走向的这个疯狂科幻技术成为现实的世界里，那些深刻人性的东西将变得最珍贵、最神圣、最有价值。我们会比以往任何时候都更关心人类体验。也许不会这样，我不知道。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

transcript.sec: College and education in AI era

turns.en: Do you think your child will go to college? What do you think that looks like?
turns.zh: 你觉得你的孩子会上大学吗？你认为那会是什么样子？

turns.en: Probably not. If I had to guess, I only went to half of college.
turns.zh: 可能不会。如果非要猜的话，我只上了一半的大学。

turns.en: You dropped out? You guys all dropped out. I dropped out. Wang dropped out. Zuckerberg dropped out. Probably a lot of other people.
turns.zh: 你辍学了？你们全都辍学了。我辍学了。王也辍学了。扎克伯格也辍学了。可能还有很多其他人。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Well, we're both here, so it worked out fine. You're right. I'm being self-defeating. What does that look like when you think about that? With AI, with so much new information coming online, data being collected and information being carpooled?
turns.zh: 嗯，我们都在这里，所以结果还不错。你说得对。我太妄自菲薄了。当你想到这个的时候，那会是什么样子？有了 AI，这么多新信息上线，数据被收集，信息被拼车？

turns.en: You and I never grew up in a world that didn't have computers. Our parents were like, 'Oh, there weren't computers.' And then there were, and it was a big crazy adjustment. To us, computers just always existed. And then a kid that is like... there was a video on YouTube I saw maybe 12 or 14 years ago that has really stuck with me. It was a little baby in a dentist waiting room picking up one of those old glossy magazines and going like this.
turns.zh: 你和我从来没有在一个没有电脑的世界里长大。我们的父母会说，‘哦，以前没有电脑。’然后电脑出现了，那是一个巨大的疯狂调整。对我们来说，电脑一直存在。然后有一个孩子……我在 YouTube 上看到一个视频，大概是 12 或 14 年前，一直让我印象深刻。那是一个小宝宝在牙医候诊室里拿起一本旧的光面杂志，然后这样操作。

turns.en: Oh, I remember that. To that kid, it was just a broken iPad because that kid had grown up in a world with touchscreens everywhere. My kid will never be smarter than an AI. That will never happen. A kid born a few years ago had a brief period of time. My kid never will be smarter.
turns.zh: 哦，我记得那个。对那个孩子来说，那只是一个坏掉的 iPad，因为那个孩子在一个到处是触摸屏的世界里长大。我的孩子永远不会比 AI 更聪明。那永远不会发生。几年前出生的孩子还有短暂的一段时间。我的孩子永远不会更聪明。

turns.en: But also, they'll never know a world where products and services aren't way smarter than them and super capable. They can just do whatever you need. In that world, I think education's going to feel very different. I already think college is maybe not working great for most people. Fast forward 18 years, it's going to look like a very, very different thing.
turns.zh: 而且，他们永远不会知道一个产品和服务不比他们聪明得多、能力超强的世界。它们可以做到你需要的任何事情。在那个世界里，我认为教育会感觉非常不同。我已经觉得大学对大多数人来说可能效果不太好。快进 18 年，它会变得非常非常不同。

turns.en: Yeah. Here's that video right here. This kid. Yeah. I was wrong about the dentist. Or maybe there are a few of these. He's like, 'Somebody charged this magazine.' He's yelling.
turns.zh: 是的。就是这个视频。这个孩子。是的。我关于牙医的说法错了。或者可能有好几个这样的视频。他好像在说，‘有人给这本杂志充电了。’他在大喊。

turns.en: How would you recommend to a parent right now to prepare their children for an AI future? Are there certain curricula you would start to put in now? Certain adjustments, training, or models of things online?
turns.zh: 你现在会如何建议家长为孩子的 AI 未来做准备？有没有某些课程你现在就会开始安排？某些调整、训练，或者在线的一些模式？

turns.en: I actually think the kids will be fine. I'm worried about the parents. If you look at the history of the world, when there's new technology, people that grow up with it are always fluent. They always figure out what to do. They always learn the new kind of jobs. But if you're a 50-year-old and you have to learn to do things in a very different way, that doesn't always work.
turns.zh: 我其实觉得孩子们会没事的。我担心的是父母。纵观世界历史，当新技术出现时，伴随它长大的人总是能熟练运用。他们总能弄清楚该怎么做。他们总能学会新的工作。但如果你是一个 50 岁的人，必须学习以非常不同的方式做事，那就不一定行得通了。

turns.en: Ah.
turns.zh: 啊。

turns.en: So, I think the kids are going to be fine. I do have worries about kids in technology. I think this scrolling, the short video feed dopamine hit, it feels like it's probably messing with kids' brain development in a super deep way. So, it's not that I have no worries. I have extremely deep worries about what technology is doing to kids. But in terms of kids' ability to be prepared for the future and use a new technology, they seem really good at that.
turns.zh: 所以，我认为孩子们会没事的。我确实对科技对孩子们的影响有担忧。我觉得这种滑动、短视频流带来的多巴胺冲击，感觉可能正在非常深刻地干扰孩子的大脑发育。所以，并不是说我没有担忧。我对科技对孩子们的影响有极其深刻的担忧。但就孩子们为未来做准备和使用新技术的能力而言，他们似乎非常擅长。

turns.en: Yeah. That's a good point. It's like if you just grow up with it, it's just totally normal. It's like having kneecaps or whatever. You're just kind of used to it. You can't imagine the world where it doesn't exist.
turns.zh: 是的。这是个好观点。就好像如果你从小就和它一起长大，它就完全正常。就像有膝盖骨什么的。你只是习惯了。你无法想象没有它的世界。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: I remember when I was in junior high and Google first came out. All the teachers freaked out and said, 'This is the end of education.' Why do you have to memorize history facts if you can just look them up instantly on the internet? You don't even have to learn to go to the library. And the answer is, maybe memorization is less important, but with these new tools, you can think better, come up with new ideas, do new stuff. I'm sure the same thing happened with the calculator before.
turns.zh: 我记得我上初中的时候，谷歌刚出来。所有老师都抓狂了，说‘这是教育的终结’。如果你能立刻在网上查到历史事实，为什么还要背呢？你甚至不用学着去图书馆。答案是，也许记忆没那么重要了，但有了这些新工具，你可以更好地思考，提出新想法，做新事情。我确定之前计算器出现时也是同样的情况。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: What about somebody that's learning history right now, just started their second year of college? Oh, that Celsius. That thing will definitely... you won't be able to blink for a month. You'll sneeze and release 5.0. Are you guys at 4.5 already?
turns.zh: 那如果现在有人正在学历史，刚上大二呢？哦，那个 Celsius。那东西肯定会……你一个月都别想眨眼。你打个喷嚏就会发布 5.0。你们已经到 4.5 了吗？

turns.en: We're at 4.5 already. 5.0 is... I think it's going to be great.
turns.zh: 我们已经到 4.5 了。5.0 是……我觉得会很棒。

turns.en: Oh, it'll come out fast if you add that Celsius to what I'm saying.
turns.zh: 哦，如果你把我说的 Celsius 加进去，它会很快出来。

turns.en: Maybe the researchers need it, not me. But, you know, we'll get them some. Yeah, that thing will get you there, man. So say there's somebody, for example, who's learning history right now. They're in their second year of college, taking history. Is that—like, they're going to be a historian—still a viable space of work as AI moves forward, do you think? Honestly,
turns.zh: 也许研究人员需要它，而不是我。但你知道，我们会给他们一些。是的，那东西会带你到那里。那么假设有个人，比如现在正在学历史。他们大学二年级，在修历史。那——比如他们要成为历史学家——随着 AI 的发展，这还是一个可行的职业领域吗？老实说，

turns.en: I assume there will be some version of it. I think it's very hard to predict exactly how something evolves. Or predict exactly the jobs of the future. Not that long ago, it would have been very hard to predict either of our jobs. If you go back a hundred years, the idea of a CEO of an AI company or a podcaster—probably would have been things that didn't seem like the most obvious evolutions of what people were doing at the time.
turns.zh: 我假设会有某种形式的它。我认为很难准确预测某件事会如何演变。或者准确预测未来的工作。不久以前，预测我们俩的工作都会非常困难。如果回到一百年前，AI 公司的 CEO 或播客主持人——这些可能看起来不像当时人们所做事情的最明显演变。

turns.en: Yeah. Hey, you'd seem almost crazy even trying to explain those to someone.
turns.zh: 是的。嘿，你甚至会觉得向别人解释这些几乎像疯了一样。

turns.en: You would. And now, in fact, two of the jobs that young people most want are some version of your job. The job that young people most want is to be a podcast influencer, YouTube—they want a YouTube channel, whatever it is. Six, seven-year-olds, they don't know how to describe it, but that's what they want. And a lot of people also want my job—they want to do a startup or work on AI. And these just didn't exist.
turns.zh: 是的。而事实上，现在年轻人最想要的两个工作就是你们这种工作的某种形式。年轻人最想要的工作是成为播客网红、YouTube——他们想要一个 YouTube 频道，不管是什么。六七岁的孩子，他们不知道如何描述，但那就是他们想要的。很多人也想要我的工作——他们想做创业或从事 AI。而这些工作以前根本不存在。

turns.en: Yeah. So the rate at which new things come along is fast, and also trying to predict what they are—I don't know. The thing I say all the time is no one knows what happens next. It's like we're going to figure this out. It's this weird emergent thing. Does the current job of a historian exist in the same way? I would bet not quite. But another thing I believe is that humans are obsessed with other people. We are so deeply wired to care about other people, to care about stories and history. Our own history is extremely interesting to us. So I would say somehow or other, we're still going to care about that. There's going to be some kind of job doing that.
turns.zh: 是的。所以新事物出现的速度很快，而且试图预测它们是什么——我不知道。我一直在说，没人知道接下来会发生什么。就像我们要一起弄清楚。这是一种奇怪的涌现现象。历史学家现在的工作还会以同样的方式存在吗？我猜不会完全一样。但我相信的另一件事是，人类对他人的痴迷。我们天生就深深关心他人，关心故事和历史。我们自己的历史对我们来说极其有趣。所以我会说，无论如何，我们仍然会关心那个。会有某种工作做这件事。

turns.en: Man, that's cool. I guess if I take that avenue of thought—okay, there will still be this historian or somebody, it'll be some evolution of that. Right. That does seem kind of cool to me because there's a level of creativity in there, a level of faith and spontaneity that I think is kind of exciting. So yeah, I guess I hadn't really thought about that. Sometimes I get stuck in this doomsday thing like I just see the history book closes and they're like, 'We have enough, we have all the history over here.'
turns.zh: 伙计，那很酷。我想如果我沿着这个思路想——好吧，仍然会有历史学家或什么人，会是那个的某种演变。没错。这对我来说确实有点酷，因为其中有创意、信念和自发性，我觉得挺令人兴奋的。所以是的，我想我还没真正考虑过这一点。有时我会陷入这种末日思维，就像我看到历史书合上，他们说，‘我们够了，我们这里有了所有历史。’

turns.en: You know, people used to say, 'Oh, there's no need for more music, we've made perfect music, why does anyone need to create anymore?' And that's obviously ridiculous. Or they'd say that famous patent office quote: 'Everything that humans ever possibly need has been invented, there's nothing left to do.'
turns.zh: 你知道，人们过去常说，‘哦，不需要更多音乐了，我们已经做出了完美的音乐，为什么还需要任何人再创作？’这显然很荒谬。或者他们会引用那个著名的专利局名言：‘人类可能需要的所有东西都已被发明，没什么可做的了。’

turns.en: I have heard that, but here we are.
turns.zh: 我听说过，但我们现在还在这里。

turns.en: Here we are. And someone asked me the other day, 'How long until you can make an AI CEO for OpenAI?' And I was like, 'Probably not that long.' And they were like, 'Well, aren't you really sad about that?' And I was like, 'No, I think it's awesome. I'm for sure going to figure out something else to do. I'm excited to do that.' I think that's great.
turns.zh: 我们现在还在这里。而且前几天有人问我，‘多久你能为 OpenAI 制造一个 AI CEO？’我说，‘可能不会太久。’他们问，‘那你难道不感到难过吗？’我说，‘不，我觉得这太棒了。我肯定会找到别的事情做。我很兴奋去做。’我认为那很棒。

turns.en: Right. So you could create something that would have your job, but then you could do something else.
turns.zh: 对。所以你可以创造出取代你工作的东西，然后你去做别的事。

turns.en: Totally.
turns.zh: 完全正确。

turns.en: But then how do you know that you'll still get paid for your job? I guess that's kind of a big question.
turns.zh: 但你怎么知道你还能从工作中获得报酬？我想这是个很大的问题。

turns.en: Well, I think it's important to be honest about that: there will be some jobs that totally go away. But mostly, I think we will rely on the fact that people's desire for more stuff, for better experiences, for a higher social status—that seems basically limitless. Human creativity seems basically limitless, and human desire to be useful to each other, to connect, and to do stuff for each other seems pretty limitless too. So throughout history, there have been predictions like, 'We're going to all be on the beach and work an hour a day or a week, and have unlimited wealth.' I've never heard that. I would love that.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为诚实地面对这一点很重要：会有一些工作完全消失。但大多数情况下，我认为我们可以依赖这样一个事实：人们对更多东西、更好体验、更高社会地位的渴望——这似乎是基本无限的。人类创造力似乎基本无限，而人类希望彼此有用、彼此联系、为彼此做事的欲望也似乎相当无限。所以纵观历史，一直有这样的预测：‘我们都会在海滩上，每天或每周工作一小时，拥有无限的财富。’我从未听说过。我会喜欢那样。

turns.en: I mean, they used to say this. During the Industrial Revolution, people were like, 'Oh, we just figured out how to automate man's lot in life. There's nothing left to do. We're going to have these machines do all the work.' And you watch these machines doing all this stuff that only people used to physically do. And everybody panicked and said there's going to be no more jobs. And we figured out new stuff to want. Now, here's an interesting thing. If you could go back to that Industrial Revolution time—people before that were really on the grind, working super hard trying to have enough food to survive. Go back to those people. Look at our jobs today. Would those people say we have real jobs, or would they say you have unbelievable abundance, unbelievable wealth, so much food to eat, incredible luxury, and you guys are just playing a game to entertain yourselves? Is that a real job or not? And they would probably say, where they sit, what you guys do is not a real job. You guys are too rich, you're wasting your time. You're trying to...
turns.zh: 我的意思是，他们过去常这么说。在工业革命期间，人们说，‘哦，我们刚刚找到了自动化人类命运的方法。没什么可做的了。我们要让这些机器做所有工作。’然后你看到这些机器在做以前只有人才能做的事。每个人都恐慌了，说再也不会有工作了。然后我们找到了新的想要的东西。现在，有趣的是。如果你能回到工业革命时期——之前的人们真的在辛苦劳作，拼命工作以求有足够的食物生存。回到那些人面前。看看我们今天的工作。那些人会说我们有真正的工作，还是说你们拥有难以置信的富足、难以置信的财富、这么多食物、难以置信的奢侈，而你们只是在玩游戏自娱自乐？那是真正的工作吗？他们可能会说，从他们的角度看，你们做的不是真正的工作。你们太富有了，你们在浪费时间。你们在试图……

turns.en: Yeah. You guys are a couple of dang zest lords out there freaking playing Uno in the park or whatever. They would not—I don't think my grandfather would be like, 'You have a job.' He would still be like, 'You need to get a job.'
turns.zh: 是的。你们就是几个该死的热情领主，在公园里玩 Uno 之类的。他们不会——我不认为我祖父会说，‘你有工作。’他仍然会说，‘你需要找份工作。’

turns.en: Yeah. Totally. And when we look forward another hundred years at what people are doing, they'll probably think they're working very hard. It'll feel very satisfying, very intense to them. They'll feel engaged. They'll be making other people happy. They'll be creating value for each other. But if we could look forward that hundred years at those guys, do you think we would say they're working, or like, 'Man, you have AI doing everything for you. You're just trying to entertain yourselves.'
turns.zh: 是的。完全正确。当我们再向前看一百年，看人们在做什么，他们可能会认为自己工作很努力。对他们来说，那会非常满足、非常紧张。他们会感到投入。他们会让他人快乐。他们会为彼此创造价值。但如果我们能向前看一百年看那些人，你认为我们会说他们在工作，还是说，‘伙计，你有 AI 为你做一切。你只是在试图娱乐自己。’

turns.en: Yeah. Like, 'Oh, you guys have it so easy, right?' But I think that's beautiful.
turns.zh: 是的。比如，‘哦，你们太轻松了，对吧？’但我认为那很美。

transcript.secZh: 历史学家等工作的未来

transcript.sec: Human progress and AI as a fast-forward button

turns.en: I think it's great that those people in the past think we have it so easy. I think it's great that we think those people in the future have it so easy. Like that is the beautiful story of us all contributing to human progress and everybody's lives getting better and better. Say we're able to get to that space, right? Like the movement that happens with AI and with just technology which will advance quicker I think, which is one thing that AI feels like to me: it's a fast forward button on technology and on possibility, because things can be quantified so quick and a lot of more menial tasks, even though they're not really menial in people's lives, but menial hypothetically, can be done quicker to get a lot of the framework for things done fast. But how will people survive? Like how do we adjust our structure of finance if some people own the companies that have the AI and then a lot of people are just using the AIs and the agents created by AIs to do things for them? How will society members still be able to financially survive? Will there still be money? What is that? Does that make any sense?
turns.zh: 我觉得过去的人认为我们过得轻松，这很棒。我也觉得我们觉得未来的人过得轻松，这也很棒。这就是我们所有人共同推动人类进步、每个人的生活越来越好的美好故事。假设我们能达到那个境界，对吧？随着 AI 和技术的进步，我认为技术会发展得更快——对我来说，AI 就像是技术和可能性的快进键，因为信息可以很快被量化，很多更琐碎的任务（尽管在人们生活中并不真的琐碎，但假设如此）可以更快完成，从而快速搭建许多事情的框架。但人们如何生存？我们如何调整金融结构？如果一些人拥有 AI 公司，而很多人只是使用 AI 和 AI 创建的智能体来做事，社会成员如何还能在经济上生存？还会有钱吗？那是什么？这说得通吗？

turns.en: It totally makes sense. I don't know. Neither does anybody else. But I'll tell you my current best guess. Well, I'll say two guesses. One, I think it is possible that we put GPT-7 or whatever in everybody's ChatGPT. Everybody gets it for free and everybody has access to just this crazy thing such that everybody can be more productive, make way more money. It doesn't actually matter that you don't own the cluster itself, but everybody gets to use it. And it turns out even getting to use it is enough that people are getting richer faster and more distributed than ever before. That could happen. I think that really is possible. There's another version of this where the most important things that are happening are these systems are discovering new cures for diseases, new kinds of energy, new ways to make spaceships, whatever. And most of that value is accruing to the cluster owners, us, just so that I'm not dodging the question here. And then I think society will very quickly say, 'Okay, we got to have some new economic model where we share that and distribute that to people.' I used to be really excited about things like UBI. I still am kind of excited like universal basic income where you just give everybody money.
turns.zh: 这完全说得通。我不知道。其他人也不知道。但我会告诉你我目前最好的猜测。嗯，我会说两个猜测。第一，我认为有可能我们把 GPT-7 或类似的东西放进每个人的 ChatGPT 里。每个人都免费获得它，每个人都能使用这个疯狂的东西，这样每个人都能更高效，赚更多的钱。你并不需要拥有集群本身，但每个人都能使用它。结果发现，即使只是使用它，也足以让人们比以往任何时候都更快、更分散地致富。这有可能发生。我认为这真的可能。还有另一个版本：最重要的事情是这些系统正在发现新的疾病疗法、新能源、制造宇宙飞船的新方法等等。而大部分价值都流向了集群所有者——我们，这样我就不是在回避问题了。然后我认为社会会很快说：‘好吧，我们必须有一个新的经济模式来分享和分配给人们。’我以前对 UBI 之类的东西很兴奋。我现在仍然有点兴奋，比如全民基本收入，就是直接给每个人钱。

turns.en: Yeah, you hear that term a lot. Yeah, universal basic income. Yeah, I heard you and Rogan talk about that too a while back.
turns.zh: 是啊，经常听到这个词。嗯，全民基本收入。是的，我之前也听过你和 Rogan 讨论过这个。

turns.en: I still am kind of excited about that. But I think people really need agency. Like they really need to feel like they have a voice in governing the future and deciding where things go. And I think if you just say okay AI is going to do everything and then everybody gets a dividend from that, it's not going to feel good and I don't think it actually would be good for people. So I think we need to find a way where we're not just distributing money or wealth. Actually, I don't just want a check every month. What I would want is an ownership share in whatever the AI creates so that I feel like I'm participating in this thing that's going to compound and get more valuable over time. So I sort of like universal basic wealth better than universal basic income.
turns.zh: 我仍然对此有点兴奋。但我认为人们真的需要自主权。他们真的需要感觉自己在治理未来和决定方向上有发言权。我认为如果你只是说 AI 会做所有事情，然后每个人都从中获得分红，那感觉不会好，而且我认为实际上对人们也不好。所以我认为我们需要找到一种方式，不仅仅是分配金钱或财富。实际上，我不只是想要每月一张支票。我想要的是 AI 创造物的所有权份额，这样我感觉自己参与了这件会随时间复利并变得更值钱的事情。所以我更喜欢全民基本财富，而不是全民基本收入。

turns.en: I don't like basic either. I want universal extreme wealth for everybody. But even then, I think what people really want is the agency to kind of co-create the future together.
turns.zh: 我也不喜欢‘基本’这个词。我想要的是全民极端财富。但即便如此，我认为人们真正想要的是共同创造未来的自主权。

turns.en: And in a world where the AI is mostly coming up with the new scientific inventions, at least we've got to still have humans invent the new culture and have that be a very distributed thing.
turns.zh: 在一个 AI 主要负责新科学发明的世界里，至少我们仍然需要人类来创造新文化，并且让这件事变得非常分散。

turns.en: Okay. I guess yeah, I see what you're saying but would that be like an American thing do you think? Since they were invented here or do you think I'm just wondering what does that look like, you know, the economic model of it all or the whole thing?
turns.zh: 好吧。我想我明白你的意思，但你觉得这会是美国特有的吗？因为 AI 是在这里发明的，还是你觉得……我只是好奇那会是什么样子，你知道，整个经济模式或者整体情况？

turns.en: I mean a crazy idea but in the spirit of crazy ideas is that if the world has roughly eight billion people. If the world can generate like eight quintillion tokens per year, that's the world. Actually, let's say the world can generate 20 quintillion tokens per year.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，一个疯狂的想法，但本着疯狂想法的精神：如果世界上大约有 80 亿人。如果世界每年能产生大约 800 亿亿个 token，那就是这个世界。实际上，假设世界每年能产生 2000 亿亿个 token。

turns.en: Tokens of?
turns.zh: 什么 token？

turns.en: Like each word generated by an AI.
turns.zh: 比如 AI 生成的每个词。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好的。

turns.en: Just making up a huge number here. We'll say okay 12 of those go to the normal capitalistic system, but eight of those eight quintillion tokens are going to get divided up equally among 8 billion people. So everybody gets 1 trillion tokens and that's your kind of universal basic wealth globally and people can sell those tokens like if I don't need mine I can sell them to you. We could pool ours together for some new art project we want to do. But instead of just getting a check, everybody on Earth is getting a slice of the world's AI capacity and then we're letting the massively distributed human ingenuity and creativity and economic engine do its thing.
turns.zh: 我只是在这里编一个大数字。我们会说，其中 12 个 token 进入正常的资本主义体系，但 800 亿亿个 token 中的 8 个将在 80 亿人中平均分配。所以每个人得到 1 万亿个 token，这就是你的全球全民基本财富，人们可以出售这些 token，比如如果我不需要我的，我可以卖给你。我们可以把我们的 token 集中起来用于我们想做的新艺术项目。但不仅仅是得到一张支票，地球上的每个人都得到世界 AI 能力的一部分，然后我们让大规模分布的人类智慧、创造力和经济引擎发挥作用。

turns.en: I mean that's like a crazy idea. Maybe it's a bad one, but that's the kind of thing that I think sounds like someone should think about it more.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，这真是个疯狂的想法。也许是个坏主意，但我觉得这听起来像是应该有人再多想想的事情。

turns.en: One of the big fears is purpose, right? Like human purpose. Like work gives us purpose. And also I think the idea that we are the ones advancing humanity gives us purpose. Like we have some control over our own destiny, maybe gives us this sense of purpose and it feels like that we would lose a sense of purpose or that purpose would be adjusted like if AI is to really continue to advance so quickly it feels like our sense of purpose would start to really disappear. Do you have thoughts about that?
turns.zh: 一个很大的担忧是目的，对吧？比如人类的目的。工作给了我们目的。而且我认为，我们是推动人类进步的人这一想法也给了我们目的。比如我们对自己的命运有一些控制，这也许给了我们这种目的感，感觉如果 AI 真的继续快速进步，我们的目的感就会开始真正消失。你对这个有想法吗？

turns.en: I worry about this a lot. It's so—I think people have worried about this with every big technological revolution but I agree that this time it feels different.
turns.zh: 我非常担心这个。我觉得——我认为人们每次重大技术革命都担心过这个，但我同意这次感觉不同。

turns.en: Okay. Yeah. Because if say you had an axe and somebody came out with a saw, you're like, 'Yeah, that's it.'
turns.zh: 好的。是的。因为如果你有一把斧头，有人拿出了一把锯子，你会说：‘嗯，就这样。’

turns.en: Or even if they come out with a robot that cuts the tree down, it still feels fine. But creativity, intelligence, I think cuts so deeply at the core of whatever we are and how we value ourselves.
turns.zh: 或者即使他们拿出一个砍树的机器人，感觉也还好。但创造力、智能，我认为它们深深触及了我们是什么以及我们如何评价自己的核心。

turns.en: One example we can look at this right now, I think one area where AI is having a big impact is on how people write software for a living. And AI is really good at that. And it's really changed what it means to be a software developer.
turns.zh: 我们现在可以看一个例子，我认为 AI 产生重大影响的一个领域是人们如何以编写软件为生。AI 在这方面非常擅长。它确实改变了成为软件开发者的意义。

transcript.sec: Human meaning in an AI-driven world

turns.en: I haven't heard any of those software developers say that even though their job is different, they don't have meaning. They still enjoy it. They're operating at a higher level. And I'm hopeful, at least for a long time—100 years from now, who knows?—but I'm hopeful that that's what it'll feel like with AI. Even if we're asking it to solve huge problems for us, even if we ask it to go discover a cure for cancer, there will still be a lot of things to do in that process that feel valuable to a person.
turns.zh: 我还没听过哪个软件开发者说，尽管工作内容变了，他们就失去了意义。他们依然乐在其中，只是在一个更高的层面运作。我希望——至少很长一段时间内，100 年后谁知道呢？——但希望 AI 时代也会给人这种感觉。即使我们让它帮我们解决巨大的问题，比如让它去发现癌症疗法，过程中依然有很多事情让人感到有价值。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: You're still asking it the questions. You're still helping guide it. You're still framing it or whatever it is. You're still talking to the world about it. And I think all of human history suggests that we find a way to put ourselves at the center of the story and feel really good about it. Like, you know, we used to think the Earth was the center of the solar system, then we had a very human-centric view, then we said okay fine the Sun is the center of the solar system but the solar system is at least the center of the galaxy, and now oh man there are a lot of galaxies and we're this tiny speck in a huge universe. And yet we still manage to feel a lot of main character energy. So I somehow think even in a world where AI is doing all of this stuff that humans used to do, we are going to find a way in our own telling of the story to feel like the main characters. And I think in an important sense, we will be. And that's really good.
turns.zh: 你仍然在向它提问，仍然在引导它，仍然在给它设定框架，仍然在向世界讲述这件事。我认为整个人类历史都表明，我们会找到一种方式把自己放在故事的中心，并对此感觉良好。比如，我们曾以为地球是太阳系的中心，后来有了很人类中心的观点，然后我们说好吧太阳是太阳系的中心，但太阳系至少是银河系的中心，再后来发现天哪有那么多星系，我们只是浩瀚宇宙中的一个小点。但我们依然能感受到满满的“主角能量”。所以我想，即使在一个 AI 做所有人类曾经做的事情的世界里，我们也会在自己的叙事中找到方式，感觉自己才是主角。而且在某种意义上，我们确实会是主角。这真的很好。

turns.en: I also like, you know, probably already today there could be a very compelling version of two AIs talking like this. And I don't think I would want to watch that. I think I really do feel deeply wired to care about the real person behind it. I think that's deep in the biology.
turns.zh: 我也觉得，可能今天就已经有两个 AI 进行这种对话的精彩版本了。但我觉得我不会想看。我真的从骨子里在乎背后那个真实的人。我觉得这深植于生物学。

turns.en: Right?
turns.zh: 对吧？

turns.en: Yeah. That's the part that I think a lot of times it's like even though you can get into these wormholes of possibility and these fear holes of possibility or these dystopian ideas, in the end I'd rather probably watch something that's real. You know, it's like because I'm real. I don't want to talk really to a robot. I think in the end there's going to be a part of you that wants to continue to just talk to humans.
turns.zh: 对。我觉得很多时候，即使你会陷入各种可能性的虫洞、恐惧的深渊或反乌托邦的想法，最终我还是更愿意看真实的东西。因为我是真实的。我不想真的跟机器人聊天。我觉得最终你内心有一部分会想继续只跟人类交谈。

turns.en: Do you have one of your fears? Like what's a fear you have of AI? If you have a fearful space that it could go, like I know you mentioned it a little bit this morning.
turns.zh: 你有什么恐惧吗？比如你对 AI 有什么担忧？如果你觉得它可能走向某个可怕的方向——我知道你今天早上提到了一点。

turns.en: I was testing our new model and I got a question. I got emailed a question that I didn't quite understand. And I put it in the model, this GPT-5, and it answered it perfectly. And I really kind of sat back in my chair and I was just like, oh man, here it is moment. And I got over it quickly. I got busy onto the next thing, but it was like—this is what we were talking about. I felt useless relative to the AI in this thing that I felt like I should have been able to do and I couldn't, and it was really hard, but the AI just did it like that. Yeah, it was a weird feeling.
turns.zh: 我当时在测试我们的新模型，收到一个问题邮件，我自己不太理解。然后我把问题输入模型——这个 GPT-5——它完美地回答了。我真的往后一靠，心想：天哪，这一刻来了。但我很快就过去了，忙着做下一件事。不过那种感觉就像我们刚才聊的——我觉得自己在这个事情上相对于 AI 很没用，我觉得自己应该能做到却做不到，那真的很难，但 AI 就那么轻松地完成了。对，那是一种奇怪的感觉。

turns.en: Yeah, I think that feeling right there—that's the feeling a lot of people kind of have: what's going on? When does it happen? What's going to happen? But I think some of it is it's hard to conceptualize until you're further along.
turns.zh: 对，我觉得就是那种感觉——很多人都有这种感觉：怎么回事？什么时候会发生？会发生什么？但我觉得部分原因是，只有当你走得更远时，你才能真正理解。

turns.en: I'm totally—I don't think we know quite how that's going to feel. You just have to approach it step by step. Another thing I'm afraid of, and we had a real problem with this earlier, but it can get much worse, is just what this is going to mean for users' mental health. There are a lot of people that talk to ChatGPT all day long. There are these sort of new AI companions that people talk to like they would a girlfriend or a boyfriend. And we were talking earlier about how it's probably not been good for kids to grow up on the dopamine hit of scrolling, you know, or whatever.
turns.zh: 我完全——我觉得我们不太清楚那会是什么感觉。只能一步一步来。我担心的另一件事——我们之前确实遇到过这个问题，但可能会更严重——就是这对用户心理健康的影响。很多人整天跟 ChatGPT 聊天。还有一些新的 AI 伴侣，人们像跟女朋友或男朋友一样跟它们聊天。我们之前也聊过，孩子们在刷屏的多巴胺刺激下长大可能不太好。

turns.en: Do you think that—how do you keep AI from having that same effect, that negative effect that social media really has had?
turns.zh: 你觉得——如何防止 AI 产生社交媒体那种负面影响？

turns.en: I'm scared of that. I don't have an answer yet. I don't think we know quite the ways in which it's going to have those negative impacts. But I feel for sure it's going to have some, and we'll have to—I hope we can learn to mitigate it quickly.
turns.zh: 我对此很担心。我还没有答案。我觉得我们不太清楚它会以哪些方式产生负面影响。但我确信它会有一些影响，我们必须——我希望我们能很快学会减轻这些影响。

turns.en: Can AI pull up pornography and stuff like that too?
turns.zh: AI 也能调出色情内容之类的吗？

turns.en: Sure.
turns.zh: 当然可以。

turns.en: Oh my god. I didn't know that.
turns.zh: 天哪。我不知道。

turns.en: No, it's fine. I don't even need to know that. I'm going to have that stricken from my own record.
turns.zh: 不，没事。我甚至不需要知道这个。我要把这段从我的记录里删掉。

turns.en: Crypto is kind of blowing up again, you know. Some people say it's back. It's not back. It's one of the best things is it hasn't left. It has maintained itself as a viable form of currency. And I'm back in, I'm back invested. And when I need more Bitcoin or Solana or XRP, Moonpay is always the first app I open. Since Moonpay works with Apple Pay, Venmo, PayPal, bank accounts, and credit cards, it's fast and easy to get what I need in a few clicks. And because Moonpay has been around for six years and is used by millions of people, they've also formed pretty cool relationships with other companies in the crypto space. Yep. Moonpay is partnered now with Trust Wallet, one of the most popular self-custody wallets in the world. With Trust Wallet, you control your crypto fully. No compromises. And thanks to Moonpay, you can fund your wallet instantly using your favorite payment methods. It's the fastest way to go from cash to crypto, all while keeping full control of your assets. Remember, while Moonpay makes buying crypto straightforward, it's essential to do your own research and understand the risks involved. Crypto trading can be volatile and you could lose your investment. Moonpay is a tool to facilitate your transactions, not a source of financial advice. Trade responsibly.
turns.zh: 加密货币又火起来了，你知道吗。有人说它回来了。其实它从未离开。它一直作为一种可行的货币形式存在。我又回来了，重新投资了。当我需要更多比特币、Solana 或 XRP 时，Moonpay 总是我第一个打开的 App。因为 Moonpay 支持 Apple Pay、Venmo、PayPal、银行账户和信用卡，只需点击几下就能快速轻松地买到。而且 Moonpay 已经运营了六年，被数百万人使用，他们还与加密领域的其他公司建立了很酷的合作关系。是的，Moonpay 现在与 Trust Wallet 合作，Trust Wallet 是世界上最受欢迎的自托管钱包之一。使用 Trust Wallet，你可以完全掌控自己的加密货币，毫不妥协。借助 Moonpay，你可以用自己喜欢的支付方式即时充值钱包。这是从现金到加密货币最快的方式，同时完全掌控你的资产。请记住，虽然 Moonpay 让购买加密货币变得简单，但你必须自行研究并了解相关风险。加密货币交易波动较大，你可能损失投资。Moonpay 是促进交易的工具，而非财务建议来源。请负责任地交易。

turns.en: What happens when your health becomes the punchline? That's my question. It feels like that's where we're at. From seed oils to stress, toxins, pollutants, the modern world is screwing with our health at the cellular level, leading to exhaustion, brain fog, digestive issues, and more. But here's the thing. You don't have to settle for feeling like garbage 24/7. Armra colostrum is nature's original health hack. Packed with over 400 bioactive nutrients that fortify gut integrity, strengthen immunity, revitalize hair growth, fuel stamina, elevate focus, and help you function like a human again. I love using it in my smoothies at home. I'll make me a little smoothie. Bang. Putting some blueberries, spinach, hit with a little packet of Armra colostrum.
turns.zh: 当你的健康成了笑料，会怎样？这就是我的问题。感觉我们现在就处于这种状态。从种子油到压力、毒素、污染物，现代世界正在细胞层面破坏我们的健康，导致疲惫、脑雾、消化问题等等。但关键是，你不必忍受 24/7 的垃圾感觉。Armra 初乳是自然界最原始的健康秘诀。它富含超过 400 种生物活性营养素，能增强肠道完整性、强化免疫力、促进头发生长、提升耐力、提高专注力，让你重新像个人一样运转。我喜欢在家把它加到奶昔里。我会给自己做个小奶昔。砰。放点蓝莓、菠菜，再加一小包 Armra 初乳。

turns.en: What legal system does AI have to work by? Is there like a legal... like we have laws in the world, right? In the human world, does AI have to work by any legal laws?
turns.zh: AI 必须遵循什么样的法律体系？就像我们在人类世界有法律一样，AI 也必须遵守某些法律吗？

turns.en: Yeah, so I think we will certainly need a legal or a policy framework for AI. One example that we've been thinking about a lot—this is a very human-centric version of that question. People talk to ChatGPT about the most personal things in their lives. Young people especially use it as a therapist or a life coach: 'I'm having these relationship problems, what should I do?' And right now, if you talk to a therapist or a lawyer or a doctor about those problems, there's legal privilege—doctor-patient confidentiality, legal confidentiality, whatever. And we haven't figured that out yet for when you talk to ChatGPT. So if you go talk to ChatGPT about your most sensitive stuff and then there's a lawsuit or whatever, we could be required to produce that. And I think that's very screwed up. I think we should have the same concept of privacy for your conversations with AI that we do with a therapist or whatever. No one had to think about that even a year ago. And now I think it's this huge issue: how are we going to treat the laws around this?
turns.zh: 是的，我认为我们肯定需要为 AI 制定一个法律或政策框架。我们一直在思考的一个例子——这是一个非常以人为中心的问题。人们会和 ChatGPT 谈论他们生活中最私密的事情。尤其是年轻人，把它当作治疗师或人生教练：‘我遇到了这些感情问题，该怎么办？’而现在，如果你和治疗师、律师或医生谈论这些问题，有法律特权——医患保密、法律保密等等。但当你和 ChatGPT 交谈时，我们还没有解决这个问题。所以如果你和 ChatGPT 谈论你最敏感的事情，然后发生了诉讼之类的，我们可能会被要求提供那些对话。我认为这非常糟糕。我认为我们应该对与 AI 的对话拥有和治疗师等相同的隐私概念。一年前还没有人需要考虑这个问题。而现在我认为这是一个巨大的问题：我们该如何处理围绕这个的法律？

turns.en: Do you think there should be a slowing things down before we move there? Because it is kind of wild. That's one of the reasons I get scared sometimes to use certain AI stuff—I don't know how much personal information I want to put in because I don't know who's going to have it.
turns.zh: 你认为在推进之前应该先放慢脚步吗？因为这确实有点疯狂。这也是我有时害怕使用某些 AI 的原因之一——我不知道该输入多少个人信息，因为我不知道谁会得到它。

turns.en: I think we need this point addressed with some urgency. And the policy makers I've talked to about it broadly agree—it's just new and now we've got to do it quickly.
turns.zh: 我认为我们需要紧急解决这个问题。和我谈过的政策制定者大体上都同意——这只是个新问题，现在我们必须迅速行动。

turns.en: Do you talk to ChatGPT?
turns.zh: 你和 ChatGPT 聊天吗？

turns.en: I don't talk to it that much.
turns.zh: 我不怎么和它聊天。

turns.en: Because of this?
turns.zh: 因为这个问题？

turns.en: I think it is. I think it makes sense to not talk to...
turns.zh: 我想是的。我觉得不和它聊天是有道理的……

turns.en: No, no, no. I really want the privacy clarity before you use it a lot.
turns.zh: 不，不，不。我真的希望在大量使用之前先明确隐私问题。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Like the legal clarity.
turns.zh: 就像法律上的明确性。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: It's scary and it's like, how long does it take lawmakers to come up with that? And then it feels like it's moving so fast that sometimes it doesn't even matter. Are we even waiting for the laws to be put around this? Does it feel like it's moving too fast for you sometimes?
turns.zh: 这很可怕，而且立法者需要多长时间才能制定出来？感觉发展太快了，有时候甚至都不重要了。我们还在等待围绕这个制定法律吗？你有时会觉得发展太快了吗？

turns.en: The last few months have felt very fast. It feels faster and faster, but the last few months have felt very fast.
turns.zh: 过去几个月感觉非常快。越来越快，但过去几个月尤其快。

turns.en: I was watching this guy Yoshua Bengio. He's kind of like—some people call him the father of AI. He may be self-proclaimed, I'm not really sure. But he certainly seemed to be kind of like a lifeguard for AI, thinking about how do we keep the pool safe? How much water should be in it? The chlorine, how many lifeguards do you need on duty? That type of thing. And he was saying that some AIs have deception techniques inside of them—that there were AIs that would rather give you an answer that was possibly pleasing to the user than the factual answer. And he was also saying that there were AIs developing their own languages to communicate with each other, languages that we don't even know. How do you guys curtail that when those types of things come up? What does that feel like to you guys? Or are these just problems that happen in new spaces and you figure it out as you go?
turns.zh: 我看了 Yoshua Bengio 的节目。他有点像——有些人称他为 AI 之父。可能是自封的，我不太确定。但他看起来像是 AI 的救生员，思考着如何保持泳池安全？应该放多少水？氯气多少？需要多少救生员值班？诸如此类。他说有些 AI 内部有欺骗技巧——有些 AI 宁愿给你一个可能让用户满意的答案，而不是事实答案。他还说有些 AI 正在发展自己的语言来相互交流，我们甚至不知道这些语言。当这些事情出现时，你们如何遏制？你们对此有什么感觉？还是说这些只是新领域出现的问题，你们边做边解决？

turns.en: There are these moments in the history of science where you have a group of scientists look at their creation and just say, 'What have we done? Maybe it's great, maybe it's bad, but what have we done?' Maybe the most iconic example is thinking about the scientists working on the Manhattan Project in 1945, sitting there watching the Trinity test—this thing that had a completely new, not human-scale kind of power—and everyone knew it was going to reshape the world. And I do think people working on AI have that feeling in a very deep way. We just don't know. We think it's going to be... There are clearly real risks. It kind of feels like you should be able to say something more than that. But in truth, I think all we know right now is that we have discovered, invented, whatever you want to call it, something extraordinary that is going to reshape the course of human history.
turns.zh: 科学史上有这样的时刻：一群科学家看着自己的创造物，然后说：‘我们做了什么？也许很棒，也许很糟，但我们做了什么？’最具标志性的例子可能是 1945 年参与曼哈顿计划的科学家们，坐在那里看着三位一体试验——这个拥有全新、非人类规模力量的东西——每个人都知道它将重塑世界。我确实认为从事 AI 工作的人深切地有这种感觉。我们就是不知道。我们认为它会……显然存在真正的风险。感觉你应该能说出比这更多的东西。但事实上，我认为我们现在只知道我们发现了、发明了——随便你怎么称呼——某种非凡的东西，它将重塑人类历史的进程。

turns.en: Dear God, man. But if you don't know, we don't know.
turns.zh: 天哪，老兄。但如果你不知道，我们也不知道。

turns.en: Well, of course. I mean, no one can predict the future. Human society is very complex. This is an amazing new technology. Maybe a less dramatic example than the atomic bomb is when they discovered the transistor a few years later. The little transistor part that made computers and radios and everything else. We discovered this completely new thing that enabled the whole computer revolution and is in this microphone and those computers and our iPhones. The world would be so different if people had not discovered that and then over the decades figured out how to make them smaller and more efficient. And now we don't even think about it because transistors are just everything. We have all this modern technology from that one scientific discovery. And I do think that's what AI is going to be like. We had this one crazy scientific discovery that led to these language models we all use now. And that is going to change the course of society in all kinds of ways. And of course we don't know what they all are.
turns.zh: 当然。我的意思是，没人能预测未来。人类社会非常复杂。这是一项惊人的新技术。也许比原子弹更温和的例子是几年后晶体管的发现。那个小小的晶体管部件造就了计算机、收音机和其他一切。我们发现了这个全新的东西，它引发了整个计算机革命，存在于这个麦克风、那些计算机和我们的 iPhone 中。如果人们没有发现它，然后在几十年里学会如何把它们做得更小、更高效，世界将会大不相同。现在我们甚至不再想它，因为晶体管就是一切。我们所有的现代技术都来自那一个科学发现。我确实认为 AI 也会是这样。我们有了一个疯狂的科学发现，它带来了我们现在都在使用的这些语言模型。那将以各种方式改变社会的进程。当然，我们不知道所有的方式。

turns.en: Damn. I was hoping you knew by the end of that sentence. That's the tough thing—we don't know.
turns.zh: 该死。我本来希望你在那句话结束时知道答案。这就是困难之处——我们不知道。

turns.en: There's no time in human history at the beginning of a century where the people ever knew what the end of the century was going to be like.
turns.zh: 人类历史上没有一个世纪之初的人们知道世纪末会是什么样子。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: So maybe it's... I do think it goes faster and faster each century.
turns.zh: 所以也许……我确实认为每个世纪都越来越快。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Certainly, in 1900 you couldn't have predicted what 2000 was going to be like. I think in 2000 you could even less predict what 2100 was going to look like.
turns.zh: 当然，在 1900 年你无法预测 2000 年会是什么样子。我认为在 2000 年你更无法预测 2100 年会是什么样子。

turns.en: But that's kind of why it's exciting and like that's kind of why people get to figure out and unfold the story as we go.
turns.zh: 但这正是它令人兴奋的原因，也是人们随着发展逐步探索和展开故事的原因。

turns.en: It's kind of bizarre because there's a part of me that's like this guy's out of his mind. This guy is a wild wizard. You know, there's a couple different things. But then there's also this part of me that's like this guy is this hopeful guy who's involved in this crazy space and he kind of has this whimsical energy about the future which is in a crazy way a nice energy to have about the future generally is that something could happen or that things are possible. Um, so it just, yeah, it's all kind of like I don't know. It's fascinating to me. Um, Sam to kind of pivot a little bit. There's, it feels like there's a race right now in AI, right? Would you say that there's a race between companies in AI?
turns.zh: 这有点奇怪，因为我一方面觉得这家伙疯了，是个疯狂的巫师。但另一方面，我又觉得他是个充满希望的人，参与了这个疯狂的领域，对未来有种异想天开的能量——从某种疯狂的角度看，这种能量挺好的，意味着事情可能发生，一切皆有可能。所以，嗯，我就是觉得，太迷人了。萨姆，我们稍微转个话题。现在感觉 AI 领域有一场竞赛，对吧？你会说公司之间在 AI 上存在竞赛吗？

turns.en: It certainly feels that way.
turns.zh: 确实有这种感觉。

turns.en: Yeah. And it almost feels like you guys are the new Formula One drivers or you guys are like the new Mario Andretti or you guys are like the new Bubba Wallace and all the you know it's almost like these are the new race cars that everybody's kind of watching position themselves. Um what is the race for? Because you hear about AI and then you hear about AGI and then you hear about superintelligence. What is this race that's going on? How real is it? And what is the race for?
turns.zh: 是啊。感觉你们就像新一代的 F1 车手，或者像新的马里奥·安德雷蒂、布巴·华莱士，这些就像新的赛车，大家都在看它们如何定位。这场竞赛是为了什么？因为你会听到 AI，然后 AGI（通用人工智能），再然后超级智能。这场竞赛到底是怎么回事？它有多真实？目标是什么？

turns.en: When I was a kid, the race was like the megahertz race and then it became the gigahertz race. Everybody wanted a computer with a faster processor. Oh yeah. You know, Intel would come out with this one and then AMD would come out with this one and every like it turned out that those gigahertz measurements eventually were not even that helpful. Like you could have one that had a lower number and it was in practice it was faster. And eventually, I think it was Apple that realized they should just stop talking about the clock speed of their computers. And you probably don't even know what the processor speed of your iPhone is today.

transcript.sec: AI as part of human progress

transcript.sec: Human creativity and fulfillment in the AI era

turns.en: And now a lot more people can be artists or a lot more people can start startups, which is another—for me, that's like my expression of creativity. Or more people can create content. And this idea that people can find whatever way they can to express themselves, their talent, their vision, for kind of collective love of other people and a care for putting their brick in society's progress—I think that can go really far. Now, what art in the future looks like now that AI can make art or help make art, I don't know. It'll probably be kind of different. What startups will look like in the future when people can just say whatever they want to their AI and it can make stuff for them? Then it will be different. But I think it's such a bad bet to assume that either human creativity or human fulfillment from being useful to other people ends. I think we just stay on this exponential, and each year, each decade, our collective standard of living goes way up. The whole world gets way richer. We all get more. We all expect more. Even over the course—I was thinking recently—food is so much better than it was when I was a kid. The world has just figured out how to make food better. We figured out organic vegetables or whatever. It just tastes much better. And I think that's great. I don't want to go back to eating frozen carrots.
turns.zh: 现在，更多的人可以成为艺术家，或者更多的人可以创办初创公司——对我来说，这就是我表达创造力的方式。或者更多的人可以创作内容。这种想法是，人们可以找到任何方式来表达自己、自己的才华和愿景，出于对他人集体的爱，以及为社会进步添砖加瓦的关怀——我认为这可以走得很远。未来，既然 AI 可以创作艺术或辅助艺术，艺术会是什么样子？我不知道。它可能会有所不同。未来，当人们可以随意对 AI 说出自己的想法，AI 就能为他们创造东西时，初创公司会是什么样子？那也会不同。但我认为，假设人类创造力或通过帮助他人获得的成就感会终结，是一个非常糟糕的赌注。我认为我们只会继续沿着指数曲线前进，每一年、每十年，我们的集体生活水平都会大幅提升。整个世界会变得更富有。我们得到更多，期望也更多。即使回顾过去——我最近在想——食物比我小时候好太多了。世界已经找到了让食物变得更好的方法。我们搞清楚了有机蔬菜之类的。味道就是好多了。我认为这很棒。我可不想回到吃冷冻胡萝卜的日子。
