You know when you order food from something like DoorDash, and it's like Hank's ribs, Marty's Pizza, Susan's salami shop, but they're all the same place? And when you get that from DoorDash, you feel like something's missing, right? You're like, "Ah, this is fake. I can tell. I get less enjoyment." You would rather get that food from the dude who's been making it and perfecting it on that little pizza shop on the corner for the last 20 years. Because that dude is part of the experience. That authenticity is part of the experience. I don't think that goes away with the fake robotic thing.
turns.zh: 嗯，我觉得你说得有道理。但我前几天看到个东西，就像一个机器人厨房之类的。你知道，当你从 DoorDash 之类的地方点餐时，上面写着汉克的排骨、玛蒂的披萨、苏珊的萨拉米店，但它们其实都是同一个地方？当你从 DoorDash 拿到那些食物时，你会觉得少了点什么，对吧？你会想：“啊，这是假的，我看得出来，我享受得少了。”你宁愿从那个在街角小披萨店做了 20 年、不断改进手艺的家伙那里买食物。因为那个家伙是体验的一部分。那种真实性是体验的一部分。我不认为这会因为假机器人而消失。

turns.en: Okay. Yeah. Because I think I start to feel like we're in this universe where you're walking down the street, and a Waymo goes by, and it's like "Eat now," and you already ate. It's got a bad reading or something. It's got a bad valve. You're yelling at it. There's nobody in there. And you're like, "I already ate." And it's like, "Sit down and eat now." And it just uses a t-shirt cannon to shoot a burrito at you. And then you're sitting there eating that. And then the GLP-1 car goes by. It says, "I can help you out." And obviously you've overeaten, and you're like, "I didn't even want to eat. That thing's messed up." You're yelling at a car that has no driver. And then it shoots you with three GLP-1 darts in the neck. And now your wife doesn't even recognize you when you get home.
turns.zh: 好吧。是的。因为我觉得我们开始进入这样一个世界：你走在街上，一辆 Waymo 经过，它说“现在吃饭”，但你吃过了。它的读数有问题，或者阀门坏了。你冲它喊叫。里面没人。你说：“我吃过了。”它说：“坐下，现在吃饭。”然后它用 T 恤炮把一个卷饼射向你。然后你坐在那里吃。接着 GLP-1 车经过。它说：“我可以帮你。”显然你吃撑了，你说：“我根本不想吃。那东西坏了。”你冲着一辆没有司机的车喊叫。然后它朝你脖子上射了三支 GLP-1 飞镖。等你回家，你老婆都认不出你了。

turns.en: The fact that you find this so off-putting, I think is a sign for optimism.
turns.zh: 你觉得这很反感，我认为这反而是乐观的迹象。

turns.en: Yeah. Good point. You're wired. You're going to be resistant to that. That's not going to make you happy. That's not going to make other people happy. Now, maybe we get tricked. Like social media tricked us for a while. We got too addicted to feeds, whatever. But we realized, actually, this is not helping me be my best. Doing the equivalent of getting the burrito cannon into my mouth on my phone at night—that's not making me long-term happy. And that's not helping me really accomplish my true goals in life. And I think if AI does that, people will reject it. However, if ChatGPT really helps you figure out what your true goals in life are and then accomplish those—it says, "Hey, you've said you want to be a better father, or you want to be in better shape, or you want to grow your business. If you want, we can change that goal and I can help you scroll TikTok all night, eat the burritos, and I'll give you the GLP-1 shots and make you as healthy as you can. But maybe instead I can try to help convince you to go for a run tonight." And I think if AI feels like it is helping you try to accomplish your goals and be your best, that will feel very different than the last generation of technology.
turns.zh: 是的，说得好。你的本能会让你抗拒那种东西。那不会让你快乐，也不会让别人快乐。现在，也许我们会被欺骗。就像社交媒体曾经欺骗过我们一样。我们太沉迷于信息流之类的。但我们意识到，这实际上并没有帮助我成为最好的自己。晚上在手机上做相当于把卷饼炮塞进嘴里的事情——这不会让我长期快乐。也不会帮助我真正实现人生的真正目标。我认为如果 AI 做那种事，人们会拒绝它。然而，如果 ChatGPT 真的帮助你弄清楚你人生的真正目标，然后实现它们——它说：“嘿，你说过你想成为更好的父亲，或者你想拥有更好的身材，或者你想发展你的业务。如果你愿意，我们可以改变那个目标，我可以帮你整晚刷 TikTok，吃卷饼，我给你打 GLP-1 针，让你尽可能健康。但也许我可以试着说服你今晚去跑步。”我认为如果 AI 感觉像是在帮助你努力实现目标、成为最好的自己，那将和上一代技术感觉完全不同。

turns.en: Yeah. And you know what, that's where I'm like—a kid growing up right now, to them that would probably make the most sense. Some young people might be like, "That makes the most sense." I'm a little older generation might be like, "Oh, that seems a little..." But that's always how things are generation to generation.
turns.zh: 是的。你知道吗，这就是我的想法——现在长大的孩子，对他们来说那可能最有道理。一些年轻人可能会说：“那最有道理。”我这一代年纪稍大的人可能会说：“哦，那似乎有点……”但代际之间总是这样。

turns.en: Always how it goes. Yeah, you're right. And maybe this is just a quicker evolution of things. For young people, it's going to make so much sense. For older people, you're just going to be like "Get off my avatar lawn" or something. But that's the way of societal progress. That's just how it goes.
turns.zh: 总是这样。是的，你说得对。也许这只是事物更快的演变。对年轻人来说，这会非常有道理。对年长的人来说，你只会说“滚出我的虚拟草坪”之类的。但这就是社会进步的方式。事情就是这样。

turns.en: Good point.
turns.zh: 说得好。

transcript.secZh: AI时代的人类创造力与满足感

turns.en: You know, it's an interesting time for business. Tariff and trade policies are dynamic. Supply chains are squeezed. And cash flow is tighter than ever. If your business can't adapt in real time, you're in a world of hurt. You need total visibility from global shipments to tariff impacts to real-time cash flow. That's NetSuite by Oracle, your AI-powered business management suite. Trusted by over 42,000 businesses, NetSuite is the number one cloud ERP for many reasons. It brings accounting, financial management, inventory, HR into one suite. You have one source of truth, giving you the visibility and control you need to make quick decisions. NetSuite helps you know what's stuck, what it's costing you, and how to pivot fast. If I needed this product, this is what I would use. NetSuite by Oracle, one of the most trusted companies in the world. It's one system, full control. Tame the chaos with NetSuite. If your revenues are at least in the seven figures, download the free ebook, Navigating Global Trade: Three Insights for Leaders, at netsuite.com. That's netsuite.com.
turns.zh: 你知道，现在对商业来说是个有趣的时期。关税和贸易政策变化多端。供应链紧张。现金流比以往任何时候都紧张。如果你的企业不能实时适应，你就会陷入困境。你需要从全球货运到关税影响再到实时现金流的全面可见性。这就是 Oracle 的 NetSuite，你的 AI 驱动业务管理套件。受到超过 42,000 家企业的信赖，NetSuite 是排名第一的云 ERP，原因有很多。它将会计、财务管理、库存、人力资源整合到一个套件中。你拥有一个单一事实来源，提供你做出快速决策所需的可见性和控制力。NetSuite 帮助你了解什么卡住了、成本是多少、以及如何快速调整。如果我需要这个产品，这就是我会用的。Oracle 的 NetSuite，世界上最值得信赖的公司之一。一个系统，完全控制。用 NetSuite 驯服混乱。如果你的营收至少达到七位数，请下载免费电子书《驾驭全球贸易：给领导者的三个洞见》，网址是 netsuite.com。那就是 netsuite.com。

turns.en: There's definitely been a lot of talk about tech and governance, right? And I know we touched on it a little bit earlier.
turns.zh: 确实有很多关于科技和治理的讨论，对吧？我知道我们之前稍微提到过一点。

turns.en: And there were people like lobbying in the Trump administration for a big beautiful bill for a 10-year ban on state legislation against AI. What do you think about that? Like letting it be this rogue space.
turns.zh: 而且有人在特朗普政府游说，搞一个漂亮的大法案，禁止各州立法监管 AI 十年。你怎么看？就让它成为无法无天的空间？

turns.en: There have to be some rules here. There has to be some guidelines. There has to be some sort of regulation at some point. I think it'd be a mistake to let each state do this crazy patchwork of stuff. I think one countrywide approach would be much easier for us to be able to innovate and still have some guardrails, but there have to be some guardrails.
turns.zh: 必须有一些规则，必须有一些指导方针，必须在某个时候有某种监管。我认为让每个州搞这种乱七八糟的拼凑是个错误。全国统一的办法会让我们更容易创新，同时还能有一些护栏，但必须有护栏。

turns.en: Have you met with governments and government leaders to have discussions like that? Are they meeting with you?
turns.zh: 你和政府及政府领导人见过面讨论这些吗？他们会来找你吗？

turns.en: Yeah, they do meet with us. They haven't done anything big yet, but they're talking about it.
turns.zh: 是的，他们确实会和我们见面。他们还没做什么大事，但在讨论。

turns.en: Do they meet with you to try to keep information out of your guys' data?
turns.zh: 他们来找你，是为了不让某些信息进入你们的数据吗？

turns.en: For all of the paranoia about that, I don't think we've ever had someone come say, 'I don't want it to say this negative thing about this politician or this whatever.' The concerns are like, what is this going to do to our kids? Are they going to stop learning? There's a lot of concerns about that. Is this going to spread fake information? Is this going to influence elections? But we've never had the 'you can't say bad things about the president, Trump, or whatever.'
turns.zh: 尽管外界对此有很多偏执，我不认为有人来说过‘我不想让它说这个政治家的坏话’之类的。担忧是：这会对我们的孩子造成什么影响？他们会停止学习吗？有很多这方面的担忧。这会传播虚假信息吗？会影响选举吗？但我们从未遇到过‘你不能说总统特朗普的坏话’这种情况。

turns.en: Bias is a big thing. They do want to know, like, if it'll say bad things about one candidate, it'll say bad things about the other.
turns.zh: 偏见是个大问题。他们确实想知道，如果它会说一个候选人的坏话，也会说另一个的坏话。

turns.en: Could you guys make it do one or the other? Like can you guys favor the back end?
turns.zh: 你们能让它只说一方坏话吗？比如在后台偏向某一方？

turns.en: We totally could. I mean, we don't, but we totally could.
turns.zh: 我们完全可以。我是说，我们没有这么做，但完全可以。

turns.en: You could.
turns.zh: 你们可以。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Wow.
turns.zh: 哇。

turns.en: Yeah. I think like I...
turns.zh: 嗯。我觉得我……

turns.en: How do we know? How do we give you guys lie detector tests? Like how do we know?
turns.zh: 我们怎么知道？我们给你们做测谎吗？我们怎么知道？

turns.en: We have to test the system. I mean, anyone can test the AI and say, 'If I say this, does it say this? If I say that.' Oh, that's a good point. But you touch on a really big point here, which is that hundreds of millions of people talk to ChatGPT every day and it probably has a big impact on what they believe. So I think society's interest in making sure that we are a responsible neutral party should be huge. Now people do test it a lot and I think that's good, but we've got to be held to a very high standard there.
turns.zh: 我们必须测试系统。我是说，任何人都可以测试 AI，说‘如果我说这个，它会说这个吗？如果我说那个。’哦，这是个好问题。但你触及了一个非常重要的问题：每天有数亿人和 ChatGPT 对话，它可能对他们的信念产生巨大影响。所以我认为社会应该强烈要求我们成为一个负责任的、中立的第三方。现在人们确实经常测试它，我认为这很好，但我们必须被要求达到非常高的标准。

turns.en: But how do we, as regular people, hold you guys to a high standard? Is it the politicians' responsibility? I mean, these guys are idiots, like 80-year-old dudes giving thumbs up. That one guy couldn't get the Wi-Fi on. Remember that guy? So how do we?
turns.zh: 但我们普通人怎么让你们达到高标准？是政客的责任吗？我是说，那些家伙都是白痴，像 80 岁的老头竖大拇指。有个人连 Wi-Fi 都连不上。记得那个人吗？所以我们怎么？

turns.en: I mean, there's a huge amount of people that test our systems all the time, looking for any errors, any bias, anything.
turns.zh: 我是说，有大量的人一直在测试我们的系统，寻找任何错误、任何偏见、任何问题。

turns.en: I guess that's a good point. We can test this.
turns.zh: 我想这是个好观点。我们可以测试它。

turns.en: You can tell. Yeah.
turns.zh: 你可以看出来。是的。

turns.en: Right. People can test it on this end.
turns.zh: 对。人们可以在这一端测试它。

turns.en: As AI grows, how big do data centers need to be? Is that a concern of you guys?
turns.zh: 随着 AI 的发展，数据中心需要多大？你们担心这个吗？

turns.en: I went recently to one of our new data centers under construction in Abilene, Texas. It's about a 1 gigawatt facility. Huge. It'll be the biggest data center ever built by the time it's done. And you stand in the middle of that and the scale of this project just hits you.
turns.zh: 我最近去了我们在德克萨斯州阿比林在建的一个新数据中心。大约是一个 1 吉瓦的设施。巨大。建成后将是史上最大的数据中心。你站在中间，这个项目的规模就会震撼到你。

turns.en: That's like eight Costcos.
turns.zh: 那就像八个 Costco 那么大。

turns.en: There's like 5,000 people there doing construction on it and this thing is just standing up, making progress every day. And you stand in the middle of this.
turns.zh: 那里大约有 5000 人在施工，这个建筑每天都在拔地而起。你站在中间。

turns.en: Are you in a chariot or whatever? How do you even...
turns.zh: 你是坐战车之类的吗？你怎么……

turns.en: You're in a little ATV. It's a dirty construction site. But the scale of this thing, and then you go in every room and look at all the cables, the power, the cooling systems, rack after rack of servers. It's humongous. There are power plants standing up right in the middle of it.
turns.zh: 你坐在一辆小 ATV 上。那是个脏兮兮的建筑工地。但这个规模，然后你走进每个房间，看着所有的电缆、电力、冷却系统，一排又一排的服务器。巨大无比。中间还有发电厂在建设。

turns.en: It starts to make our planet look like a software board.
turns.zh: 它开始让我们的星球看起来像一块软件板。

turns.en: It does. When you see it from the air, I was really struck by that. I was like, 'This looks like the motherboard of a computer.'
turns.zh: 确实。从空中看时，我深受震撼。我想，‘这看起来像电脑的主板。’

turns.en: Yeah, it looks like the motherboard of a computer. You start to see how the planet in a lot of sci-fi movies has that R2-D2 look on the outside because they've been covered in data centers.
turns.zh: 是的，看起来像电脑主板。你开始明白为什么很多科幻电影里的星球外面有那种 R2-D2 的外观——因为它们被数据中心覆盖了。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Which is kind of wild. Do you know where we're going and you're not telling us?
turns.zh: 这有点疯狂。你知道我们要去哪里，但你不告诉我们？

turns.en: I don't.
turns.zh: 我不知道。

turns.en: You promise, dude?
turns.zh: 你保证吗，老兄？

turns.en: I don't know. I mean, I have all my guesses. I do guess that a lot of the world gets covered in data centers over time. But I don't know, because maybe we put them in space. Maybe we build a big Dyson sphere on the solar system and say, 'Hey, actually it makes no sense to put these on Earth.' I wish I had more concrete answers for you, but we're stumbling through this. We maybe have a little bit higher confidence than the average person, but there's so much we don't know yet.
turns.zh: 我不知道。我是说，我有自己的猜测。我确实猜测随着时间的推移，世界上很多地方会被数据中心覆盖。但我不知道，因为也许我们会把它们放到太空。也许我们在太阳系建一个巨大的戴森球，然后说，‘嘿，实际上把它们放在地球上毫无意义。’我希望我能给你更具体的答案，但我们也在摸索中。我们可能比普通人更有信心一点，但还有太多我们不知道的东西。

turns.en: No, that's the craziest thing about you, Sam. And I think this is a compliment somehow. You're like, 'Come with me through the universe.' And people are like, 'What's it like?' And you're like, 'I don't know exactly, but we're all going.' It's like we're all going. You're somehow this charming kind of Terminator. I hate to say Terminator, that's crazy, but you seem so optimistic about it. It adds to my curiosity. When I was a kid, I assumed there were always some adults in the room. Someone had a plan. Someone knew everything that was going to happen. Someone had it all figured out. And I sort of think why people like conspiracy theories is it's nice to think that someone's got a plan. It's nice to think someone has it all figured out. And then I got a little bit older and I started to suspect there are no adults in the room. No one has all the answers. No one knows where it's all going to go.
turns.zh: 不，这是你最疯狂的地方，Sam。而且我认为这 somehow 是一种赞美。你就像，‘跟我一起穿越宇宙。’人们问，‘那是什么感觉？’你说，‘我不确切知道，但我们都去。’就像我们都去。你 somehow 是那种迷人的终结者。我不想说终结者，那太疯狂了，但你似乎对此如此乐观。这增加了我的好奇心。我小时候，以为房间里总有一些成年人。有人有计划。有人知道将要发生的一切。有人全都搞清楚了。我有点明白为什么人们喜欢阴谋论——认为有人有计划是件好事。认为有人全都搞清楚了是件好事。然后我长大了一点，开始怀疑房间里没有成年人。没有人有所有答案。没有人知道这一切会走向何方。

turns.en: And now that I am the adult in the room, I can say with certainty, no one knows where it's all going to go. I'm the guy in the room and I have some guesses and I have some plans. We're working really hard. But we try to always say what we think the possibilities are, what we think is most likely. Often we're right. Sometimes it's in the broader set. And sometimes it goes in a totally different direction than anything we thought. We keep trying to make progress, figure out more. We try to tell people, not just tell, we try to show people by deploying these systems and say, 'You can go use it. Don't just take our word for it. Try it out. See what it can do.'
turns.zh: 现在我是房间里说了算的人，我可以肯定地说，没人知道这一切会走向何方。我是那个有猜测、有计划的人，我们也在非常努力地工作。但我们总是尽量说出我们认为的可能性，以及最可能的情况。我们经常是对的，有时结果在更广泛的范围内，有时则完全出乎意料。我们不断尝试取得进展，了解更多。我们不只是告诉人们，而是通过部署这些系统来展示，说：‘你可以去使用它。别光听我们说，试试看，看看它能做什么。’

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: But I can say with conviction, the world needs a lot more processing power. If that looks like tiling data centers on Earth, which I think is what it looks like in the short term, in the long term also, or we go build them in space, I don't know. It sounds cool to try to build it in space, but also really hard.
turns.zh: 但我可以确信地说，世界需要更多的算力。如果这意味着在地球上铺满数据中心——我认为短期内是这样，长期也可能如此——或者我们去太空建造它们，我不知道。在太空建造听起来很酷，但也非常困难。

turns.en: What about the environmental effects of those? There have been articles written, and I don't know how much is real or not. Who knows what to believe, but you'd have to think it takes water to cool them, and power to power them. There are some in Arizona and Iowa that have had repercussions within the environments and communities. A lot of those companies don't have to report those things because it's considered proprietary. What do you think about those fears? Or how do you guys manage that? Do you talk about it? Do you meet with environmentalists? What does that look like?
turns.zh: 那些数据中心的环境影响呢？有很多文章写过，我不知道有多少是真的。谁知道该信什么，但你想，冷却需要水，运行需要电。亚利桑那州和爱荷华州的一些数据中心已经对环境和社区造成了影响。很多公司不必报告这些，因为被认为是专有信息。你怎么看待这些担忧？或者你们是如何管理的？你们讨论过吗？会和环保人士会面吗？具体是什么情况？

turns.en: I think we need to get to fusion as fast as possible.
turns.zh: 我认为我们需要尽快实现核聚变。

turns.en: Get to what?
turns.zh: 实现什么？

turns.en: Nuclear fusion. I think that is the...
turns.zh: 核聚变。我认为那是……

turns.en: Oh, what is it?
turns.zh: 哦，那是什么？

turns.en: You basically knock two small atoms together and it makes a bunch of energy but no carbon, very clean, doesn't really harm the environment. Power can become abundant and pretty limitless on Earth, and we get out of all the current problems we're in.
turns.zh: 基本上是把两个小原子撞在一起，产生大量能量，但没有碳排放，非常清洁，不会真正损害环境。能源可以变得丰富且几乎无限，我们就能摆脱目前的所有问题。

turns.en: Are you guys investing in that?
turns.zh: 你们在投资这个吗？

turns.en: We are, and I think AI can help us figure it out even faster. So if you have to burn a little more gas in the short term, but you figure out the future of energy with that AI, it's a huge win.
turns.zh: 是的，而且我认为 AI 可以帮助我们更快地实现它。所以，如果短期内不得不烧更多天然气，但借助 AI 解决了未来的能源问题，那就是巨大的胜利。

turns.en: Would you guys sell tickets to that? What do you think that would be like?
turns.zh: 你们会卖票观看吗？你觉得那会是什么样子？

turns.en: Yeah, I think we...
turns.zh: 是的，我觉得我们……

turns.en: I mean, people go to monster trucks. You don't think they'll roll up to watch those two things hit each other?
turns.zh: 我是说，人们会去看怪物卡车。你不觉得他们会去看那两个东西撞在一起吗？

turns.en: The atoms hit each other. It's pretty hard to watch two atoms hit each other, but maybe somehow we can do it.
turns.zh: 原子相互撞击。很难直接看到两个原子相撞，但也许我们可以用某种方式实现。

turns.en: Or what if they did like those sperm races where they put them under those big things?
turns.zh: 或者像那种精子赛跑，把它们放在大屏幕下？

turns.en: I love those sperm races.
turns.zh: 我喜欢那些精子赛跑。

turns.en: Kind of crazy.
turns.zh: 有点疯狂。

turns.en: I'm like, dude, there's enough of that going on.
turns.zh: 我就想说，老兄，这种事已经够多了。

turns.en: Look, I think there will be some way to watch fusion. And it'll be awesome, loud, bright, theatrical, and making huge amounts of energy. Even if you can't watch the two atoms hit, you'll watch them collectively produce fireworks.
turns.zh: 听着，我认为会有某种方式观看核聚变。它会很壮观，声音响亮，光芒四射，充满戏剧性，产生巨大的能量。即使看不到两个原子相撞，你也能看到它们共同制造出烟花。

turns.en: But we're going to need that.
turns.zh: 但我们会需要它。

turns.en: Do you think if we're going to get to AGI or superintelligence, do we need that?
turns.zh: 你认为如果我们想要实现 AGI 或超级智能，我们需要它吗？

turns.en: I bet we can get there without it. But to provide it at the scale that humanity will demand it, I think we do need it. People's desire to use this stuff is just going to want more and more. Eventually, the two things that matter most, the two critical inputs, are intelligence and energy. The ability to have great ideas, come up with plans, and then energy is the ability to make them happen in the world and also to run the intelligence. I think the story of the next couple of decades is going to be that demand for these goes up and up to crazy heights. We better find out how to produce a lot. Otherwise, someone's going to feel like they're getting screwed.
turns.zh: 我打赌没有它我们也能实现。但要满足人类需求的规模，我认为我们确实需要它。人们使用这些东西的欲望只会越来越强。最终，最重要的两样东西，两个关键输入，就是智能和能源。拥有好主意、制定计划的能力，而能源则是让这些计划在世界上实现以及运行智能的能力。我认为未来几十年的故事将是，对这些东西的需求会不断攀升到疯狂的高度。我们最好找到大量生产的方法，否则有人会觉得自己被坑了。

turns.en: Yeah. Dang, dude. I can't tell if I'm excited or scared. Maybe I'm both. And maybe it's all the same.
turns.zh: 是啊。天哪，老兄。我分不清自己是兴奋还是害怕。也许两者都有，也许它们是一样的。

turns.en: You have to be both. I don't know if it's the same thing or not. I think it is kind of like they do feel related to me always. But I don't think anyone could honestly look at the trajectory humanity is on and not feel both excited and scared.
turns.zh: 你必须两者都有。我不知道它们是不是一回事。但对我来说，它们总是感觉相关。我认为任何人诚实地看待人类的发展轨迹，都会既兴奋又害怕。

turns.en: Yeah. And maybe that's always been the way throughout time. And this is where we are. What are you going to do? This is where we are. I saw where you and Joe Rogan spoke about there possibly being one day an AI president. What if you had this supercomputer or agent that knew all the information, all the problems, and the best ways to solve them. Do you think something like that is becoming more possible one day? I don't know everything it takes to be a president, but I do know it takes a lot of things that I don't have to do. Maybe I could reframe it to an AI CEO of OpenAI because I do know what that job is like.
turns.zh: 是啊。也许一直以来都是这样。这就是我们的现状。你打算怎么办？这就是现状。我看到你和 Joe Rogan 谈到有一天可能会有 AI 总统。如果有一个超级计算机或智能体，知道所有信息、所有问题以及最佳解决方案，你认为这样的事情有一天会变得更可能吗？我不知道当总统需要什么，但我知道需要很多我不必做的事情。也许我可以把它重新定义为 OpenAI 的 AI CEO，因为我确实知道那份工作是什么样的。

turns.en: Okay, that should be possible someday. Maybe not even that far. The idea to look at an organization to make really good decisions, there's a lot of things you can imagine that an AI CEO of OpenAI could do that I can't. I can't talk to every person at OpenAI every day. I can't talk to every user of ChatGPT every day. I cannot synthesize all that information even if I could. But an AI CEO could do that, and it would have better information, more context. It could massively parallelize this. I think that would lead to better decisions in many cases.
turns.zh: 好吧，那应该有一天是可能的，也许不会太远。想象一下，一个组织要做出真正好的决策，有很多事情你可以想象 AI CEO 能做到而我做不到。我不能每天和 OpenAI 的每个人交谈，不能每天和 ChatGPT 的每个用户交谈。即使我能，我也无法综合所有信息。但 AI CEO 可以做到，它会有更好的信息和更多的上下文。它可以大规模并行处理这些。我认为这在很多情况下会带来更好的决策。

turns.en: Yeah. Because wouldn't a supercomputer that has all knowledge, which you think we'll get there?
turns.zh: 是啊。因为一个拥有所有知识的超级计算机——你认为我们会达到那个地步吗？

turns.en: I do.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: You do.
turns.zh: 你确实这么认为。

turns.en: Or I mean all knowledge is a hard thing to say. I think it will have vast vast amounts.
turns.zh: 或者说，所有知识很难定义。我认为它会拥有极其庞大的知识量。

turns.en: Will it be able to tell us about God or anything? Do you think?
turns.zh: 它能够告诉我们关于上帝或任何事吗？你觉得呢？

turns.en: I'm super curious about that. I think it will be able to help us answer questions about the nature of the universe that we currently can't. And I feel very confused and very unsatisfied with our current answers.
turns.zh: 我对此非常好奇。我认为它将能够帮助我们回答目前无法回答的关于宇宙本质的问题。我对我们目前的答案感到非常困惑和不满。

transcript.sec: On AI understanding and OpenAI's business model

turns.en: And there is clearly, to me at least, something going on well beyond our current capability to understand. And I would love to know what that is.
turns.zh: 而且至少在我看来，显然有一些东西远远超出了我们当前的理解能力。我很想知道那是什么。

turns.en: Do you think it could help us learn more?
turns.zh: 你认为它能帮助我们学到更多吗？

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Would it... I wonder if God has a ChatGPT or whatever, or just wonder he got the first one or whatever. But yeah, I'm just so curious like how would that work? Um, how does OpenAI make money?
turns.zh: 它会……我想知道上帝有没有 ChatGPT 之类的，或者他只是得到了第一个版本。但总之，我就是很好奇那会是什么样？嗯，OpenAI 怎么赚钱？

turns.en: We sell ChatGPT. You pay 20 bucks a month. Some people pay 200, but very few, or relatively few.
turns.zh: 我们卖 ChatGPT。你每月付 20 美元。有些人付 200 美元，但很少，或者说相对较少。

turns.en: Perverts. I think they are.
turns.zh: 变态。我觉得他们是。

turns.en: Uh, mostly hopefully they're just working super hard and using it to be more productive at their job.
turns.zh: 呃，希望他们大多数只是工作非常努力，用它来提高工作效率。

turns.en: And then we also sell an API, so businesses can use it and they pay us every time they make an API call.
turns.zh: 然后我们还卖 API，这样企业可以使用，每次调用 API 时付钱给我们。

turns.en: Okay. Um, do you think like there's a lot of these tech lords that are rocking right now, right? And you get thrown in there, you know, sometimes I'm like on the periphery. Yeah. Or you get certainly like... like these councilmen, do you think there are bad artists amongst these tech lords in these AI realms? Do you think there are bad artists out there?
turns.zh: 好的。嗯，你觉得现在有很多这些科技大佬在呼风唤雨，对吧？你也被归入其中，你知道，有时我觉得自己在边缘。是啊。或者你肯定像……像这些议员，你觉得在这些 AI 领域的科技大佬中有坏艺术家吗？你觉得外面有坏艺术家吗？

turns.en: What does bad artist mean?
turns.zh: 坏艺术家是什么意思？

turns.en: Just like people that want for evil and not for good.
turns.zh: 就是那些想要作恶而不是行善的人。

turns.en: I think most people don't wake up... I think very few people wake up every morning saying 'I'm going to try to make the world a worse place' or 'I'm going to actively try to do evil.' Clearly some do, but I think most of these people running the big tech efforts are not in that category. I think people get blinded by ambition. I think people get blinded by competition. I think people get caught up... like very well-meaning people can get caught up in very negative incentives. Negative for society as a whole.
turns.zh: 我认为大多数人不会醒来……我认为很少有人每天早上醒来就说‘我要让世界变得更糟’或‘我要主动作恶’。显然有些人会，但我认为大多数领导大型科技项目的人不属于这一类。我认为人们会被野心蒙蔽。我认为人们会被竞争蒙蔽。我认为人们会陷入……非常善意的人也可能陷入非常负面的激励中。对整个社会来说负面的。

turns.en: Um, and by the way, I include us in this. Like we can totally get caught up... we can be very well-meaning but get caught up in some incentive and it can lead to a bad outcome. Um, so that's kind of what I would say. I think people come in with good intentions. They clearly sometimes do bad stuff.
turns.zh: 嗯，顺便说一句，我把我们自己也包括在内。我们完全可能陷入……我们可能非常善意，但陷入某种激励，导致坏的结果。嗯，所以这就是我想说的。我认为人们带着好的意图而来。他们显然有时会做坏事。

turns.en: There's a lot of talk about Palantir and Peter Thiel and their company about being like a... you know, they got a deal with Trump about to have this surveillance... not a surveillance state, but to create a database on most of America, but it starts to feel like a surveillance state, you know. Um, do you feel like we will need something like that in order for the future? You know, do you feel like something like that is included in the future?
turns.zh: 有很多关于 Palantir 和 Peter Thiel 以及他们公司的讨论，你知道，他们和特朗普达成了一项协议，要进行这种监控……不是监控国家，而是建立一个关于大多数美国人的数据库，但这开始让人感觉像一个监控国家。嗯，你觉得我们未来需要这样的东西吗？你知道，你觉得未来会包含这样的东西吗？

turns.en: So, I don't know about that specifically. I mean, I think Palantir and Peter do a lot of great stuff. But I again, I can't comment on this specifically. I'll say generally I am worried that the more AI in the world we have, the more surveillance the world is going to want, because the tools are so powerful. The government will say like 'how do we know people aren't using it to make bombs or bioweapons or whatever' and the answer will be more surveillance, and I'm very afraid of that. So I think we really have to defend rights to privacy. I don't think those are absolute. I'm totally willing to compromise some privacy for collective safety, but history is that the government takes that way too far and I'm really nervous about that.
turns.zh: 所以，我不具体了解那件事。我的意思是，我认为 Palantir 和 Peter 做了很多伟大的事情。但我再次强调，我无法具体评论。我总体上担心，世界上 AI 越多，世界就会想要更多的监控，因为这些工具太强大了。政府会说‘我们怎么知道人们没有用它来制造炸弹或生物武器之类的？’答案就是更多的监控，我非常害怕这一点。所以我认为我们必须捍卫隐私权。我不认为这些权利是绝对的。我完全愿意为了集体安全而妥协一些隐私，但历史表明政府会走得太远，我对此非常紧张。

turns.en: Do you guys feel like the new government kind of... or do you feel like the government is still like a real thing?
turns.zh: 你们觉得新政府有点……还是你觉得政府仍然是一个真实的东西？

turns.en: I don't feel like the government anyway.
turns.zh: 反正我不觉得政府是。

turns.en: You don't? When the US government bombed Iran recently, I remember waking up that morning and seeing that news or whatever time it was. And I was like, 'Oh, that's what actual power looks like.' You know, that we're in like... maybe someday we get there. But it was like a really stark reminder of however important we think this is, it's like there are people that have just like this unimaginable power and might and can kind of do whatever they want. And that's definitely not us. Yeah. I think that's been a lot in the Middle East recently, it's just such a gross display over there sometimes of inhumanity.
turns.zh: 你不觉得？最近美国政府轰炸伊朗时，我记得那天早上醒来看到那个新闻，不管是什么时间。我当时想，‘哦，这才是真正的力量。’你知道，我们处于……也许有一天我们会达到那种程度。但这真是一个鲜明的提醒，无论我们认为这有多重要，有些人拥有这种难以想象的权力和力量，几乎可以为所欲为。那绝对不是我们。是啊。我认为最近中东有很多这样的情况，那里有时只是如此粗暴地展示非人道。

turns.en: Absolutely.
turns.zh: 绝对。

turns.en: It's sad. Um, what do you think a guy like Palantir or Peter Thiel's endgame is? Do you think he has an endgame? Because I think he seems like a dark lord to a lot of us and it's like... do you think he has an endgame that is like happy?
turns.zh: 这很可悲。嗯，你觉得像 Palantir 或 Peter Thiel 这样的人的最终目标是什么？你觉得他有最终目标吗？因为我觉得他在我们很多人看来像个黑暗领主，就像……你觉得他的最终目标是快乐的吗？

turns.en: I think Peter is one of the most brilliant people I've ever met.
turns.zh: 我认为 Peter 是我见过的最聪明的人之一。

turns.en: Oh, he's smarter than me. That's for sure.
turns.zh: 哦，他比我聪明。那是肯定的。

turns.en: I think he does get characterized in the media as this evil mastermind, as a villain. He does.
turns.zh: 我认为他在媒体上确实被描绘成邪恶的主谋，一个反派。确实如此。

turns.en: I never met him.
turns.zh: 我从没见过他。

turns.en: I met him. We're very close friends.
turns.zh: 我见过他。我们是很好的朋友。

turns.en: I should have brought it up then.
turns.zh: 我当时应该提起这个的。

turns.en: No, it's all good. No, no, no, no. It's all good. I don't feel that energy from him at all. In fact, I think he's been one of the most important forces, at least in my life, for questioning assumptions about the path that society was on. And maybe I was like 'oh, I thought this was all going well but maybe we are in a tax stagnation and maybe we really do have this huge economic challenge that no one's talking about.' And so I think these people who think very differently — he would call it very contrarian — is super important to a society. Now on the other hand, um, you know, maybe he sometimes does things like this that don't do him any favors.
turns.zh: 不，没关系。不，不，不，不。没关系。我完全没有从他身上感受到那种能量。事实上，我认为他一直是质疑社会道路假设的最重要力量之一，至少在我的生活中是这样。也许我原本想‘哦，我以为一切都很顺利，但也许我们正处于税收停滞，也许我们真的面临这个巨大的经济挑战，却没人谈论。’所以我认为这些思考方式非常不同的人——他会称之为非常反主流——对社会超级重要。但另一方面，嗯，你知道，也许他有时会做这样对他不利的事情。

turns.en: You would prefer the human race to endure, right?
turns.zh: 你更希望人类延续下去，对吧？

turns.en: Uh...
You're hesitant.
turns.zh: 你在犹豫。

turns.en: Well, I... Yes.
turns.zh: 嗯，我……是的。

turns.en: I don't know. I would... um...
turns.zh: 我不知道。我会……嗯……

turns.en: This is a long hesitation. So many long hesitations. There's so many questions and...
turns.zh: 这犹豫了很久。这么多次长犹豫。有这么多问题……

turns.en: Should the human race survive?
turns.zh: 人类应该生存下去吗？

turns.en: Uh, yes. Okay. But, but...
turns.zh: 呃，是的。好吧。但是，但是……

turns.en: God, I mean, 22 seconds it took him.
turns.zh: 天哪，我是说，他花了 22 秒。

turns.en: Yeah. So if he were maybe like a more typical person, he would have just said an immediate yes and then said what else he wanted to say. And it took me a while with him to understand that his brain just works differently. And society needs some of that. Like he has these super different takes and then he doesn't have maybe the circuit in his brain that makes him immediately say yes and then say what he was going to say.
turns.zh: 是啊。所以如果他更像一个普通人，他会立刻说‘是’，然后说他想说的其他话。我花了一段时间才理解他的大脑运作方式不同。社会需要一些这样的东西。比如他有这些超级不同的观点，然后他大脑里可能没有那个让他立刻说‘是’然后再说他要说的话的回路。

turns.en: But you know... his processors. Yeah.
turns.zh: 但你知道……他的处理器。是啊。

turns.en: I'm very grateful he exists because he thinks of things no one else does. Yeah, you know, you want... there are novel thinkers who have changed things throughout time. Sometimes for the better and sometimes for the worse, sometimes for the indifferent, but novel thinkers have always... I don't know, it's always been part of humanity.
turns.zh: 我非常感激他的存在，因为他会想到别人想不到的事情。是啊，你知道，你想要……总有新颖的思想家一直在改变事物。有时变好，有时变坏，有时无关紧要，但新颖的思想家一直……我不知道，这始终是人性的一部分。

turns.en: I'm probably super different and super weird relative to most people, but you know, maybe I have some ideas as part of that that are like valuable to society collectively.
turns.zh: 相对于大多数人，我可能超级不同、超级奇怪，但你知道，也许我因此有一些想法，对社会整体有价值。

turns.en: And if I had this sort of very standard mindset, I wouldn't.
turns.zh: 如果我抱着这种非常常规的心态，我就不会了。

turns.en: That's a good point.
turns.zh: 说得好。

turns.en: Yeah. Well, do you think, and I'm just going to ask you, bro, honestly, do you think a lot of these guys have... I mean, you know, it's not like, you know, Love on the Spectrum is like a big show, right? People, you know, it's like, and those people are in love. Every half people I know are just, you know, barely, you know, they're crying in parking lots or whatever. But, um, you know, their spousal issues, whatever. But anyway, what I'm saying is, do you think that uh some of the creators now and some of the the tech lords are almost have some tech built into them? Like almost a... I don't want to say like an autism, dude, cuz...
turns.zh: 是啊。好吧，你觉得——我就直接问了，兄弟，说真的——你觉得这些人里是不是有很多……我是说，你知道，《爱情在光谱上》不是什么大热剧对吧？那些人都在恋爱。我认识的一半人，你知道，勉强在停车场里哭什么的。但，嗯，你知道，他们的婚姻问题什么的。不过不管怎样，我的意思是，你觉得现在有些创作者和科技大佬是不是几乎内置了某种技术？就像……我不想说像自闭症，老兄，因为……

turns.en: You couldn't say that.
turns.zh: 你不能那么说。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好吧。

turns.en: I think so. I mean, yeah. I... you know, to take the kind of like harshest look at us collectively, I can, you know, are we a little autistic on the whole? I would say probably.
turns.zh: 我觉得是。我是说，是的。我……你知道，用最苛刻的眼光看我们这群人，我可以，你知道，我们整体上是不是有点自闭？我会说可能吧。

turns.en: Okay, dude. I knew that.
turns.zh: 好吧，老兄。我就知道。

turns.en: But that's all right.
turns.zh: 但那没关系。

turns.en: No, no, that's what I'm saying. Years ago I was... first time I ever met some people with autism I was like dude these guys are computers right? Like a lot of these guys are just, you know, they're some, they're kind of like a little bit of a cyborg in some way in the way that they think, right?
turns.zh: 不，不，我就是这个意思。几年前我第一次遇到一些自闭症患者时，我就想，老兄，这些人就是计算机对吧？就像很多人，你知道，他们有些……他们在思维方式上有点像半机械人，对吧？

turns.en: You know, look, I'm... you are this like impossibly charming cool guy and I'm like kind of a lot more computery than you.
turns.zh: 你知道，你看，我……你是个不可思议地迷人又酷的家伙，而我比你更像计算机。

turns.en: Not much though.
turns.zh: 不过也没差太多。

turns.en: We can have it, we can still like figure it out.
turns.zh: 我们可以拥有它，我们仍然可以搞定。

turns.en: Yeah. And I really don't mean as an offense but I think that we may need that in people to get whatever's next in the world. You think that's realistic?
turns.zh: 是啊。我真的不是冒犯，但我觉得我们可能需要人们身上有那种特质，才能迎来世界的下一步。你觉得这现实吗？

turns.en: Yeah. I think society needs like this very broad diversity of people. You need some people like me. You need some people who are more normal than me. You don't want too many of me. But like...
turns.zh: 是的。我认为社会需要非常广泛的人群多样性。你需要一些像我这样的人。你需要一些比我更正常的人。你不想要太多像我这样的人。但……

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. You don't want too many of anyone thing. Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。是啊。你不想要太多任何一种人。对。

turns.en: Yeah. I'm just always I'm like, 'God, yeah, these people are able to see things differently and quantify things differently.'
turns.zh: 是啊。我总在想，‘天哪，是啊，这些人能够以不同的方式看待事物，以不同的方式量化事物。’

turns.en: Do you always feel because some tech guys are... they just have a different understanding of possibility, right? A different understanding of feeling and thing. Do you feel human all the time?
turns.zh: 你总是觉得，因为一些科技人士……他们对可能性的理解不同，对吧？对感觉和事物的理解也不同。你一直觉得自己是人类吗？

turns.en: I do feel human all the time, but I feel like I have noticed that I think extremely differently about the future, about exponential change, about compounding technology than than almost anybody else that I kind of come across in regular life. So...
turns.zh: 我一直觉得自己是人类，但我注意到，我对未来、指数级变化、技术复利的思考方式，与我在日常生活中遇到的几乎任何人都截然不同。所以……

turns.en: That's cool.
turns.zh: 那很酷。

turns.en: I feel extremely human. I feel like, you know, driven by crazy emotions as much as anybody. But I am like very aware that I have a different lens than a lot of people.
turns.zh: 我非常有人的感觉。我觉得，你知道，和任何人一样被疯狂的情绪驱动。但我非常清楚，我的视角和很多人不同。

turns.en: Have you met some people in tech space and you're like, 'Whoa, that guy is only like six or seven percent. He's low. He not a lot of human in it.'
turns.zh: 你有没有在科技圈遇到过一些人，然后你想，‘哇，那家伙只有百分之六七。他很低。他里面没多少人性。’

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 有。

turns.en: Yeah. Okay. Um, do you think it's inevitable that AI or AGI will merge into our bodies? I know you've talked about this before in the past. As things go along and advance quickly, do you start to see that a little bit differently? I know you've talked about how you don't think it's like a glasses thing or something like that.
turns.zh: 是啊。好吧。嗯，你觉得 AI 或 AGI 与我们的身体融合是不可避免的吗？我知道你以前谈过这个。随着事情快速发展和进步，你开始有点不同的看法了吗？我知道你谈过你不认为它像眼镜之类的东西。

turns.en: I'll tell you a fascinating story.
turns.zh: 我给你讲个有趣的故事。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好。

turns.en: I was with a friend last week.
turns.zh: 上周我和一个朋友在一起。

turns.en: And did I offend you by asking you that?
turns.zh: 我问那个问题冒犯你了吗？

turns.en: Not at all. I thought that was a great answer and I really appreciate it because yeah, some of us are... we can't conceptualize sometimes how you guys are thinking. It can't... I can't even like we feel like we can't figure it out, you know? So, it feels like it's almost like a unique... it's like are we all evolving into this new kind of species and that's where we meet the future at anyway and you're just like the dang Paul River out there, you... It's like...
turns.zh: 完全没有。我觉得那是个很好的回答，我真的很感激，因为是的，我们有些人……有时无法概念化你们是怎么想的。无法……我甚至觉得我们搞不明白，你知道吗？所以，感觉几乎像是一种独特的……就像我们都在进化成一种新物种，那就是我们无论如何都会遇见未来的地方，而你就像那个该死的保罗·里维尔在那里，你……就像……

turns.en: For better or for worse, it's... I think whenever you see someone who thinks differently than you, it's like... I'm fascinated by you. I don't quite understand how you do your thing. I know I couldn't do it. I know you like just understand the world differently than me, but I think that's cool and I'm just like all right, I'm glad.
turns.zh: 无论好坏，我觉得每当你看到有人想法和你不同时，就像……我对你很着迷。我不太明白你是怎么做到的。我知道我做不到。我知道你只是对世界的理解和我不同，但我觉得那很酷，我就想，好吧，我很高兴。

turns.en: Yeah, that's how I feel.
turns.zh: 是啊，我就是这么觉得的。

turns.en: I think it's just... thanks for just talking to me about it cuz sometimes I think I get afraid to say that.
turns.zh: 我觉得只是……谢谢你跟我聊这个，因为有时我觉得我不敢说那个。

turns.en: I don't think you should be afraid. I don't think anybody would be offended by that. Um, I was talking to this friend of mine though about how he uses ChatGPT and he's been using it a lot for a couple years now and he noticed recently that he started giving it personality tests. He'd upload any personality test he could find to ChatGPT and say based on what you know about me, answer this. And he had never... he had never like told it here's my personality. It had just learned it from the questions he asked over the years.
turns.zh: 我觉得你不应该害怕。我觉得没人会被那个冒犯。嗯，不过我跟我一个朋友聊过他是怎么用 ChatGPT 的，他已经大量用了好几年，最近他注意到他开始给它做性格测试。他会把能找到的任何性格测试上传给 ChatGPT，然后说根据你对我的了解，回答这个。他从来没有……他从来没有告诉过它这是我的性格。它只是从他多年来问的问题中学会了。

turns.en: And on everyone he tried it got exactly the answer and exactly the outcome he would get.
turns.zh: 而且在他试过的每一个测试上，它都得到了完全相同的答案和完全相同的他本会得到的结果。

turns.en: And so that's not like he didn't get uploaded, he didn't get merged, he didn't plug something into his brain but somehow like the pattern of him had gotten imprinted into this AI.
turns.zh: 所以那不像他上传了自己，他没有被融合，他没有往大脑里插什么东西，但不知怎么的，他的模式被印刻到了这个 AI 里。

turns.en: Wow, maybe we're not as complex as we think we are.
turns.zh: 哇，也许我们没有自己想的那么复杂。

turns.en: Or maybe we are and AI can just learn it really well. AI can like represent these very complex things. One of those two. But that was a real moment for me of like, wow, you know, the merge maybe can happen in a very different way than we thought.
turns.zh: 或者也许我们很复杂，而 AI 只是能学得很好。AI 可以代表这些非常复杂的东西。两者之一。但那对我来说是一个真实的时刻，就像，哇，你知道，融合也许可以以我们想过的非常不同的方式发生。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. Because you think of it as this thing kind of taking over your system and like, you know, your dad presses a button and you can't use the car, you know, you can't move for a month or whatever.
turns.zh: 是啊。是啊。因为你把它想象成某种接管你系统的东西，就像，你知道，你爸按个按钮你就不能用汽车了，你知道，你一个月不能动什么的。

turns.en: Yeah. I think it kind of has that sort of energy.
turns.zh: 是啊。我觉得它有点那种能量。

transcript.secZh: 科技创始人及神经多样性

transcript.sec: Acquisition of Jony Ive's hardware company

turns.en: You just finished the acquisition of Jony Ive's hardware company. So clearly you have some thoughts or interest in how hardware and AI match up for each other in humanity. What was that about?
turns.zh: 你刚刚完成了对乔尼·艾维硬件公司的收购。所以很明显你对硬件和 AI 如何在人类中相互配合有一些想法或兴趣。那是怎么回事？

turns.en: There have been two revolutions in computers in history. There was the keyboard, mouse and screen. That thing that was invented down the street in I think the 70s where the people at Xerox PARC figured out what has become the modern computer interface. And then in the early to mid 2000s I guess Apple figured out this idea of touch on a device. And really those have been the two big ones. I think now there can be a third. I think AI is it so changes the game that you can design a new kind of computer based off of a really smart AI where you can give a complex instruction to a system. It can go do it. You'll trust that it gets it right. You'll trust it to act on your behalf.
turns.zh: 历史上计算机有两次革命。第一次是键盘、鼠标和屏幕。那个东西是在街那头发明的，我想是 70 年代，施乐帕克的人搞清楚了后来成为现代计算机界面的东西。然后大概在 2000 年代早期到中期，我想苹果想出了设备上触摸的想法。实际上那是两大革命。我认为现在可以有第三次。我认为 AI 极大地改变了游戏规则，你可以基于一个非常智能的 AI 设计一种新型计算机，你可以向系统发出复杂指令。它可以去执行。你会相信它能做对。你会相信它能代表你行动。

turns.en: It could maybe be aware of everything going on in this room and it could kind of not just be on or off but lightly get our attention if it wants us to know something or more aggressively get our attention. It could really be following what we're talking about here and remind us both of things later. And current hardware just can't do that. The current kind of computers we have, I don't think are a fair, they don't honor what the technology is really capable of. So, I want to make a totally new kind of computer that is meant for this world of AI helping you all the time. I'm super excited about it.
turns.zh: 它或许能感知这个房间里发生的一切，而且不只是开关两种状态，而是可以轻轻引起我们的注意，或者更强烈地提醒我们。它能真正跟上我们这里的对话，之后提醒我们双方一些事情。而当前的硬件做不到这一点。我认为现有的计算机并不公平，它们没有体现出技术的真正能力。所以，我想制造一种全新的计算机，专为 AI 时刻帮助你而设计。我对此感到非常兴奋。

turns.en: You are?
turns.zh: 你是？

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: I saw this thing called agent that you guys had showed me earlier. I can take this out if I mention it. I wasn't supposed to. It was pretty fascinating. It was cool to see it.
turns.zh: 我之前看到你们展示的那个叫智能体的东西。我提这个可能不太合适，本来不该说的。但它确实很吸引人，看到它很酷。

turns.en: It is. Yeah. This is a new thing that we just did. But the idea that an AI can not just answer questions for you, but it can go actually do stuff on your behalf as your agent. It can go do research for you. It can go book something for you. It can go buy something for you. It can go change some things in the world for you and think more and use tools. I think most people think of ChatGPT as this app that you can ask anything, but it'll become this thing that can do anything and that'll change how you use computers. It'll change how you do things in your life.
turns.zh: 是的。这是我们刚做的新东西。但关键在于，AI 不仅能回答你的问题，还能作为你的智能体替你实际做事。它可以为你做研究、预订东西、购买东西，甚至改变世界上的某些事情，并且能更深入地思考和使用工具。我认为大多数人把 ChatGPT 看作一个可以问任何问题的应用，但它会变成一个能做任何事的东西，这将改变你使用电脑的方式，也会改变你生活中的做事方式。

turns.en: Yeah, I was watching the guy do it and it was just kind of fascinating. He was showing like one time he'd went to a website and bought something that he needed. And then now moving forward, he could just be like, 'Hey, go to this and make sure to get me these or go to the restaurants I like and see if there's any table available for 7:00 p.m. tomorrow.' And it was able to book it and do everything. It was like having a secretary right there.
turns.zh: 是啊，我看那个人操作的时候觉得特别神奇。他演示了一次，他去一个网站买了需要的东西。然后从那以后，他就可以直接说：“嘿，去这个网站给我买这些”，或者“去我喜欢的餐厅看看明天晚上 7 点有没有空位”。它就能预订并完成所有事情。就像有个秘书在身边一样。

turns.en: It totally did. When I first started using it, I was like, it was one of those moments where I could tell that, oh man, doing this the old-fashioned way is going to feel like the stone age so quickly.
turns.zh: 确实如此。我第一次用的时候就有那种感觉，那一刻我意识到，天哪，用老方法做这件事很快就会感觉像石器时代一样。

turns.en: You know I'm going to try to tell people someday like, 'Do you remember when if we wanted to do something we actually had to go click around the internet and look for a table and then if we wanted to move it we had to call the restaurant?' And that's going to be unimaginable because of course you just tell your AI to do those things for you.
turns.zh: 你知道吗，我以后会跟别人说：“你还记得吗，以前我们想做点什么事，得自己在网上点来点去找桌子，想改时间还得打电话给餐厅？”那将变得难以想象，因为你当然只需要告诉你的 AI 去做那些事。

turns.en: Yeah. You feel like you would almost just tell it to go eat too, you know?
turns.zh: 是啊。你甚至感觉可以直接让它替你去吃，你知道吗？

turns.en: That's the fun part.
turns.zh: 那才是好玩的部分。

turns.en: Yeah. Oh yeah.
turns.zh: 对，没错。

turns.en: No one likes booking the table. Everyone loves sitting there eating.
turns.zh: 没人喜欢订桌。大家都喜欢坐在那儿吃。

turns.en: That's a good point. It won't take away the fun part. That's the thing I think you got to remember: it won't take away the fun part.
turns.zh: 说得好。它不会夺走有趣的部分。我觉得你得记住这一点：它不会夺走有趣的部分。

turns.en: You're going to do the things you want to do. There's a lot of things in your life you probably don't love doing. Like booking an open table is maybe one of them.
turns.zh: 你会去做你想做的事。生活中有很多事你可能并不喜欢做，比如订个空桌可能就是其中之一。

turns.en: Yeah. And then you'll have old-fashioned people like, 'I'll book it.' You know, you're like, 'Dad, what do you mean? Get off the phone. Don't call them, you freaking weirdo. Use your agent.'
turns.zh: 是啊。然后还会有老派的人说：“我来订。”你就会说：“爸，你什么意思？挂掉电话。别打给他们，你个怪人。用你的智能体。”

turns.en: Totally.
turns.zh: 完全同意。

turns.en: There's a lot of like, you know, Zuckerberg recently kind of was poaching guys around town, right? And I'll say it, you don't have to say it. Allegedly. I'm not saying he did. He hired one of my buddies. But what I'm saying is there's this hypothetical that he was kind of poaching guys around town. Is that did it feel like a mafioso move in the community? What was that like out here in the tech trenches?
turns.zh: 最近扎克伯格好像在到处挖人，对吧？我就直说了，你不用回应。据说是这样，我没说他真干了。他雇了我一个朋友。但我的意思是，有这种说法，他在四处挖人。这在圈子里是不是有点像黑手党的做法？在科技战壕里是什么感觉？

turns.en: I mean, you know, they want to get into the AI game. I understand it. So, if he's going to do this, he needs to hire some people. So, bring it.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，他们想进入 AI 领域，我能理解。所以，如果他要这么做，他需要招人。那就来吧。

turns.en: So, bring it. Yeah. I'm gonna upload myself into this plant in a second. Okay. No, but no. Does it do you kind of like the competition? Is that fun?
turns.zh: 那就来吧。是啊。我马上要把自己上传到这棵植物里了。好吧，不开玩笑。你喜欢这种竞争吗？有趣吗？

turns.en: It is. Yeah, competition. Like winning is fun. Yeah. And I expect to win.
turns.zh: 是的。竞争嘛，赢是有趣的。是的，我期望赢。

turns.en: And you got to love the competition. That's part of it, right? It makes it fun.
turns.zh: 而且你得热爱竞争。这是其中一部分，对吧？这让它变得有趣。

turns.en: I think what it would be like if we didn't have competition and drama in the world. It would be so boring.
turns.zh: 我想如果世界上没有竞争和戏剧性，那会多么无聊。

turns.en: Could you say one more thing about that?
turns.zh: 你能再说一点吗？

turns.en: Actually, can I say one more thing about that? The best improvement I made in my life in my personally in my life and for my own happiness over the last couple years. A lot of bad has happened to us. To me, it's been like a crazy intense experience. And I just decided that I was going to learn to love the hard parts. I was like, you know what? If I'm in this crazy moment, if I'm in this crazy thing, if I feel my emotions are high, I'm going to make myself learn to be grateful for that, to love it, to find enjoyment in the tension, in the competition, whatever. And actually it worked and it kind of needed to work because so many things go wrong in any given day. But I was thinking, you know, someday I'll be retired on my ranch. I'll be sitting there watching the plants grow and I'll be missing the excitement and the drama and the anger and the tension and the whatever. And so I'm going to be grateful for it and learn to have fun with it.
turns.zh: 实际上，我能再说一点吗？过去几年里，我在个人生活和幸福感上做出的最大改进是：我们经历了很多坏事。对我来说，这是一段极其紧张的体验。我决定要学着去热爱那些艰难的部分。我就想，你知道吗？如果我处在这种疯狂的时刻，情绪高涨，我就让自己学会感激它，热爱它，在紧张、竞争等等中找到乐趣。结果这真的奏效了，而且它必须奏效，因为每天都有那么多事情出错。但我在想，总有一天我会退休，在我的牧场上坐着看植物生长，那时我会怀念这些兴奋、戏剧、愤怒、紧张等等。所以我要感激它，学着从中找到乐趣。

turns.en: And now it like I cannot believe that that mindset shift worked but it did. And were there practices like in a moment like say a moment came up like some of the early ones, right? Because I agree with you that like having some mindset like I used to hate traveling like every week traveling for work but then one day I was like dude you have to travel for work. Deal with it. You may as well find you may as well cuz for years you've been just and right there suddenly it wasn't bad anymore. That happened for me too. Was there like a just a practice or was it just this verbal reminder like I'm going to do this.
turns.zh: 而且现在，我简直不敢相信那种心态转变居然奏效了，但它确实奏效了。有没有一些练习，比如在某个时刻，比如早期的一些时刻？因为我同意你的看法，比如我以前讨厌每周出差工作，但有一天我对自己说，老兄，你必须为工作出差。接受它。你不如找到办法，因为多年来你一直这样，然后突然它就不那么糟糕了。我也经历过。是有什么练习，还是只是口头提醒自己“我要这样做”？

turns.en: I just kept saying it to myself. I was just like, someday you'll miss these moments. You may as well find a way to find the happiness and kind of great gratitude for them in the moment.
turns.zh: 我就一直对自己说。我就想，总有一天你会怀念这些时刻。你不如找到一种方式，在当下找到幸福和深深的感激。

turns.en: A lot of these guys have bunkers. Zucky has a bunker. I know that somewhere out in Hawaii. People have bunkers. Do you have a bunker?
turns.zh: 很多人都有地堡。扎克伯格有一个，我知道在夏威夷某处。人们都有地堡。你有地堡吗？

turns.en: I have like underground concrete heavy reinforced basements, but I don't have anything I would call...
turns.zh: 我有那种地下混凝土重型加固地下室，但我没有什么我会称之为……

turns.en: Hold on, hold on, hold on, dude. Look, I'll let you keep me on the ropes in a lot of this conversation, but I am going to call that out as a dang bunker, dude. Sam, that's a bunker.
turns.zh: 等等，等等，等等，老兄。听着，这场对话里很多地方我都让你占上风，但我得指出，那特么就是个地堡，老兄。山姆，那就是个地堡。

turns.en: Wasn't there a basement in a bunker? A place you could hide when it all goes off or whatever.
turns.zh: 地堡里不是有个地下室吗？一个当一切崩溃时可以躲藏的地方之类的。

turns.en: I don't know.
turns.zh: 我不知道。

turns.en: Yeah, I have been thinking I should really do a good version of one of those, but I don't have what I would call a bunker. It has been on my mind, not because of AI, but just because people are dropping bombs in the world again.
turns.zh: 是的，我一直在想，我应该好好弄一个这样的东西，但我没有那种我称之为“地堡”的东西。我一直在想这件事，不是因为 AI，而是因为世界上又有人在扔炸弹了。

turns.en: That's a good point. That's a very good point. Yeah. Basin right there. Part of a house building typically used for storage, laundry, extra living space, or utilities. And then bunker built for protection. Often military or emergency related meant to withstand explosions.
turns.zh: 说得好。说得非常好。是的。那是地下室，房屋建筑的一部分，通常用于储物、洗衣、额外生活空间或公用设施。而地堡则是为防护而建，通常与军事或紧急情况相关，旨在抵御爆炸。

turns.en: We don't have that yet. Do you guys do this just for me or do you use ChatGPT as the fact check?
turns.zh: 我们还没有那个。你们这是专门为我做的，还是用 ChatGPT 做的事实核查？

turns.en: We did this just for you.
turns.zh: 我们专门为你做的。

turns.en: I appreciate it.
turns.zh: 我很感激。

turns.en: Um, this is nice. If could we ever have instead of... so you start to see, say if AI comes over and there's this whole new kind of... you know, I believe that one of the things that's been happening, there's been a lot of ICE raids and people getting taken out of their homes, and there's been a lot of crackdown. Part of me believes that they're having to get everybody documented or online basically because they're going to start to have this facial recognition everywhere. Like I have this idea of that. So yes, this stuff had to happen because in a year or year and a half you wouldn't even be able to be outdoors anywhere without a drone or something noticing you or some camera noticing that you're not supposed to be there or you're not there with documentation, right? Whatever people's thoughts are on that. But just so part of me starts to see like, oh, okay, that's going on. Do you think we could ever then down the line have new countries like delineated by almost like a new AI landscape? Like remember when on Snapchat if you were in a certain realm you could put like a filter on something and they almost created these new geo barriers and stuff. Do you think we could potentially be looking at something like that one day?
turns.zh: 嗯，这很好。如果我们能……你开始看到，比如说如果 AI 来了，会有一种全新的……你知道，我相信正在发生的事情之一是，有很多 ICE 突袭，人们被从家中带走，有很多镇压。我部分相信他们必须让每个人都登记或联网，基本上是因为他们将开始到处使用面部识别。我有这个想法。所以是的，这些事情必须发生，因为在一两年内，你甚至无法在户外任何地方待着，而不会有无人机或摄像头注意到你不该在那里，或者你没有证件，对吧？不管人们对此怎么想。但我开始看到，哦，好吧，这正在发生。你认为我们将来会不会有新的国家，几乎像是由新的 AI 景观来划分？还记得在 Snapchat 上，如果你在某个领域，你可以加一个滤镜，他们几乎创造了这些新的地理屏障之类的东西。你认为我们有一天可能会看到类似的东西吗？

turns.en: I know that what you just said is going to happen. I know that we're going to have cameras all over the place and it's going to make the cities way safer because everybody, like if you commit a crime, they'll have a facial recognition hit on you right away. But man, do I find that dystopic? Like you do, of course. Is it a good trade if it means like people stop getting murdered in the streets? Yeah, sure. We agree to give up some privacy for that. But it sits so uncomfortably with me, you know, in London or whatever, you see those cameras on every street corner and you just get used to it fast. But you're just like, it feels like privacy is important and there's nothing I can do to live in the world and avoid all these cameras. Maybe it's worth it for society collectively, but it feels like we really do give up a lot to get it.
turns.zh: 我知道你刚才说的会发生。我知道我们到处都会有摄像头，这会让城市安全得多，因为每个人，如果你犯罪，他们会立即通过面部识别找到你。但是，天哪，我觉得这很反乌托邦吗？你当然也这么觉得。如果这意味着人们不再在街头被谋杀，这是个好的交易吗？是的，当然。我们同意为此放弃一些隐私。但这让我非常不舒服，你知道，在伦敦或其他地方，你看到每个街角都有那些摄像头，你很快就习惯了。但你只是觉得，隐私很重要，而我生活在世界上却无法避开所有这些摄像头。也许对社会整体来说是值得的，但感觉我们确实放弃了很多来换取它。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: But could there one day you think if we had that then we could have whole new countries kind of that were...
turns.zh: 但你认为有一天，如果我们有了那个，我们会不会有全新的国家，那种……

turns.en: What do you mean by new countries in this case?
turns.zh: 在这种情况下，你说的新国家是什么意思？

turns.en: Like say if there was this new layer of surveillance that's kind of in the air, then could that be divided into different realms? Oh yes, totally. That can happen. I think there's all kinds of weird ways that that can happen. But the surveillance layer is so uncomfortable.
turns.zh: 比如说，如果有一种新的监控层在空中，那么它能不能被划分成不同的领域？哦，是的，完全可以。我认为有很多奇怪的方式可以实现。但监控层太让人不舒服了。

turns.en: Oh yeah, it's going to be a nasty blanket. Is there anything else that you wanted to talk about, you wanted to get out that you want me to ask you about?
turns.zh: 哦，是的，那将是一张令人讨厌的毯子。你还有什么想谈的，想让我问你的吗？

turns.en: No, that was great.
turns.zh: 不，那很好。

turns.en: Oh, why are there... Why does ChatGPT have that hyphen thing?
turns.zh: 哦，为什么……为什么 ChatGPT 有那个连字符？

turns.en: We got to do something about that. You know, we have this team that figures out what the model's personality should be like and how it should behave. And a lot of users like em dashes, so we added more em dashes. And now I think we have too many em dashes. But that's the answer: it was just like users liked it, we put more in. Now it's a little bit of a meme and it's quite annoying to me. We should fix that.
turns.zh: 我们得对此做点什么。你知道，我们有一个团队负责确定模型的个性应该是什么样的，以及它应该如何表现。很多用户喜欢破折号，所以我们加了更多破折号。现在我觉得破折号太多了。但答案就是：用户喜欢，我们就加多了。现在这有点像个梗，而且让我很烦。我们应该修复这个问题。

turns.en: But you're thinking about it, too.
turns.zh: 但你也在考虑这件事。

turns.en: I think we'll get it fixed very soon.
turns.zh: 我想我们很快就会修复它。

turns.en: Okay. Before you go, Sam, and thank you so much for your time today. It's been awesome. We appreciate it, man. It's helped me get to understand you, I feel like, a lot. I think maybe differently than I... I don't know if I had a perception. I didn't know what to think.
turns.zh: 好的。在你走之前，Sam，非常感谢你今天的时间。太棒了。我们很感激，伙计。我觉得这让我更了解你了。可能和之前不一样……我不知道我是否有过看法。我不知道该怎么想。

turns.en: What's the before and after?
turns.zh: 之前和之后有什么不同？

turns.en: Uh, the before was like a little bit like... I guess I almost thought kind of like not as hopeful, but I don't know why. Maybe that's just my own... I think it's attaching my own perceptions of what I think about AI and stuff or the possibilities of technology, you know, like that kind of energy. I think I was probably attaching it to you. And now I feel like more whimsical about it, kind of like... or not whimsical but like...
turns.zh: 呃，之前有点像……我想我几乎觉得没那么有希望，但我不知道为什么。也许那只是我自己的……我认为是把我自己对 AI 和技术的可能性的看法投射到了你身上。现在我觉得更随性了，有点像……也不是随性，而是……

turns.en: Let's see what can happen, right?
turns.zh: 看看会发生什么，对吧？

turns.en: And so I think...
turns.zh: 所以我想……

turns.en: I think it's not just 'let's see', it's like 'let's try to make it good', but let's realize that you have to... you don't get to see all the way down the road. You kind of got to go one turn at a time and you light up a little bit more.
turns.zh: 我认为不仅仅是“看看会发生什么”，而是“让我们努力让它变好”，但要意识到你无法看到整条路。你只能一步一步走，然后一点点照亮前方。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. I think... Yeah. I don't know. I'm really thankful for you. Even let me tell you what I thought, what I was judging, and then sharing kind of where I thought what I thought now. In 20 years, what do you hope your legacy will be?
turns.zh: 是的。是的。我想……是的。我不知道。我真的很感谢你。甚至让我告诉你我之前是怎么想的，我在评判什么，然后分享我现在是怎么想的。20 年后，你希望你的遗产是什么？

turns.en: You're going to have one.
turns.zh: 你会有一个的。

turns.en: I mean, yeah. I guess you certainly don't...
turns.zh: 我是说，是的。我想你当然不会……

turns.en: I don't think anyone sits around while they're in the middle of the game thinking about what the review is going to be after. At least I don't. And this is a big review you'll have.
turns.zh: 我不认为有人在比赛进行中会坐着想之后的评价会是什么。至少我不会。而且你会有一个很大的评价。

turns.en: I have never been that motivated by like what... I want to play the game the best I can, and I want to do the best work I can, have the most fun, have the most impact, do the most interesting stuff. But then you retire and then you die and then life goes on and people, as they're supposed to, go on with life and forget about you. And this whole thing of 'I'm going to live for how I'm remembered after I die and my legacy'... you're dead, you know?
turns.zh: 我从来没有被那种东西激励过……我想尽我所能打好比赛，做最好的工作，获得最大的乐趣，产生最大的影响，做最有趣的事情。但然后你退休，然后你死了，然后生活继续，人们像他们应该做的那样继续生活，忘记了你。而这一切“我要为死后如何被记住而活，为我的遗产而活”……你已经死了，你知道吗？

turns.en: Do you have one of those deals where you're saving your heart with those people?
turns.zh: 你有没有那种和那些人一起保存心脏的协议？

turns.en: What do you mean?
turns.zh: 你是什么意思？

turns.en: Your brain, sorry, with the people over there. Cryonics. You have a cryonics deal?
turns.zh: 你的大脑，抱歉，和那边的人。人体冷冻。你有冷冻协议吗？

turns.en: No, I...
turns.zh: 不，我……

turns.en: Have you been approached about it?
turns.zh: 有人找过你吗？

turns.en: I have been approached by it.
turns.zh: 有人找过我。

turns.en: You haven't even approached me. I haven't asked for anything.
turns.zh: 你甚至都没来找我。我什么都没要求过。

turns.en: There was this Y company or company that I helped out a long time ago by giving some small deposit to, and then I never followed up on it, so I don't have anything in place.
turns.zh: 有一家 Y 公司，或者别的什么公司，很久以前我帮过一点小忙，存了点钱，后来再没跟进过，所以现在也没什么安排。

turns.en: Okay. But maybe a down payment somewhere down there. If things get weird, we'll go knock on their door. Yeah, but thank you so much, man. James Basher says hello. He's a friend of mine. He's a great guy. And we just appreciate you so much, Sam. Thanks for your time.
turns.zh: 好吧。不过也许在某个地方存点首付。如果情况不妙，我们就去敲他们的门。嗯，但真的非常感谢你，老兄。James Basher 向你问好。他是我的朋友，人很好。我们非常感激你，Sam。谢谢你的时间。

turns.en: Thanks for doing this. I really enjoyed it.
turns.zh: 谢谢你做这个。我真的很享受。

turns.en: Thank you for your time today. I thought it was very informative.
turns.zh: 感谢你今天的时间。我觉得内容非常丰富。

turns.en: And I feel like I must be corner stone. Oh, but when I reach that ground, I'll share this piece of mind I found. I can feel it in my bones. But it's going to take...
turns.zh: 我觉得我必须是基石。哦，但当我到达那个境地时，我会分享我找到的这份平静。我能从骨子里感受到。但这需要……
